Body HTML: 10mm Density Cube Set (no display case): indium, scandium, calcium, silver, manganese, strontium, barium, hafnium and the whole set of the remaining rare earths from lanthanum to lutetium

Title: 30ml Plastic Tube
Body HTML: 30ml plastic tube, 2.5×4cm, with aluminum screwcap

Title: Gadolinium
Body HTML:
This is our first gadolinium cube, another member of the rare earth group of metals. While remarkably unremarkable so far, gadolinium's prospects of breaking out into fame and fortune brightened considerably when it was discovered that the metal makes for an ideal ingredient in magnetic refrigerators. Yes, some day the old noisy compressor fridge in your kitchen might be replaced with one that runs on whisper-quiet, environmentally friendly gadolinium!

For this entry we’ll select a nice piece of pure gadolinium showing the fibrous knots as shown - a beautiful texture!

Title: Neodymium
Body HTML:
Neodymium magnets have made this otherwise obscure element popular and these are found in many devices today. Even though the name might make it sound that the magnets are neodymium they're really only a minor component in otherwise ordinary iron magnets but the Nd is the secret sauce that turns it into a much stronger magnet.

Pure neodymium is a delicate metal that goes from shiny to tarnished in minutes and left out in air it will completely corrode in a matter of days into a pile of powdery dust. For this reason keeping it shiny requires encapsulation in glass with argon gas.

Title: Erbium
Body HTML:
Erbium is one of the pricier elements of the rare earth group. The lanthanide series takes up a whole row on the periodic table and all are very similar chemically which makes their separation into pure metals a maddeningly difficult task. In erbium's case it also happens to fall on the rarer side which explains the price. A small sample of very pure (99.99%) erbium however is a pretty affordable entry for your collection of elements!

Five grams as shown, other sizes available also.

Title: Antimony
Body HTML: A metalloid. A not quite metallic enough metal. They sure *look* like metal.... except when you drop it and it shatters like glass! Antimony would be one good example of this (though it's not quite as fragile as glass). The element however crumbles easily and is a skin irritant. All the more reason to appreciate its intricate crystalline structure in this handy container containing little pieces of 99.9999% pure Sb.

Title: Ytterbium
Body HTML:
The town of Ytterby near Stockholm, Sweden, yielded the chemistry world with ores rich in mysterious new elements. Named after its namesake town, ytterbium is one of the rare earth metals misnamed for not actually being all that rare but infamous for the tremendous difficulty needed to separate it from its neighbors in this group. 

For no reason other than its weird name, ytterbium is a popular first choice for those looking to expand their collection into the mysterious rare earths group. Here we include a few grams of this slightly yellow and exotic metal.

Title: Chlorine
Body HTML:
As deadly as it is beautiful, chlorine's characteristic yellow hue turns to a viscous, deep yellow-orange liquid when pressurized to over 1000(!) Psi, as in this little ampoule. If you were to heat this cube in boiling water (and, uh, we're not saying you should) you would cross the supercritical liquid-vapor point and the liquid would appear to vanish. After cooling down it would suddenly condense back out into a half and half state as in the photo. One of our most attractively displayed offerings.

This is a small ampule containing pressurized chlorine in gel revealing its intense yellow color.

Title: Terbium
Body HTML:
Few people have heard the name "terbium" and fewer still have seen it - and of those who have seen this metal only a handful have actually held a chunk of it in their hands. Now for a very reasonable price you can join this elite crowd of nerds who collect esoterica :-) Terbium is a member of the Rare Earth metals group, so-called less because they're all that rare than because they're extremely difficult to isolate and, as such, you Rarely See These Metals in everyday life!

Here we've put a generously sized piece of 99.9% pure terbium in a round acrylic container ready to be displayed in your collection!

Title: Tin
Body HTML: Sure, tin isn't the sexiest metal but it's harder to find than you might think given that "tin cans" are really made from steel. Actually, very few household items contain much of this metal nowadays. This is a sample of ultrapure 99.99% electrolytically refined tin metal casting sputters attractively packaged in the round plastic container as shown; ready for a display case or on its own!

Title: Beryllium
Body HTML: Element number 4 on the periodic table happens to be the lightest structural metal and it would be an absolutely fantastic option if it wasn't so outrageously rare and expensive. A few years ago a hi-tech firm took the trouble to make a bike frame out of beryllium which cost an astounding $25,000 each. In solid form the metal is lighter than aluminum but much harder and stiffer. It would be possible to make a skyscraper ten times taller than the Empire State if it was made of Be. The metal however is toxic when shaved down to dust as it lodges in the lungs causing a disease that thankfully very few people need to worry about since, again, the metal is too rare and valuable to be used in anything outside of very specialized applications.

Title: Dysprosium
Body HTML:
Like the other metals in the rare earths group, dysprosium is usually separated from its kin via time consuming processes that drive up the cost of getting the isolated element. Also as in the rest of these cases, the type that is most often available will be fibrous-looking pieces that form as crystals precipitated out of solution - and it's the end stage of the lab purification process before being shipped off to the customer.

As shown in this photo, this piece of dysprosium weighs five grams.

Title: Thallium
Body HTML:
Thallium kills. It's an order of magnitude more poisonous than arsenic and just 3 grams of the stuff, less than the weight of a penny, is all that it takes to kill a person. But behind this bullet-proof window called Lucite the little sample within is safe enough that a baby can play with it. Not that that's a good idea, mind you! In addition to being poisonous, thallium is also a finicky metal that is difficult to keep free of surface oxides, becoming leaden gray under oil and degrading to dust in open air. 

Tucked in this plastic container is a small piece of pure thallium kept free of oxides thanks to being encapsulated in a small glass ampule with argon gas.

Title: Chromium
Body HTML: Everyone's familiar with chromium as "chrome", that super shiny quintessence of what metal should be. Of course, what they're looking at is only a very thin electrolytically deposited layer and oftentimes it ain't even real chromium but aluminum (pshaw!!). Since chromium tends to be brittle at high purity it acts closer to glass than metal. For this reason, and also because it's a skin irritant to some people, you won't find it being used in its pure, solid form commercially. But a collector can still get their hands on the real deal right here with this very pure sample of chromium metal as shown.

Title: Carbon
Body HTML: Carbon is so interesting. It is found in its humble form of coal and its coveted form of diamonds. Allotropes present the collector of elements with a choice as they have to pick one or the other or shoehorn two or more types into the slot. In the "humble" form carbon has several further sub-choices and you can pick any color as long as it's black! Amorphous, crystalline, glassy and nanotubes can all be selected as representing the sixth element on the periodic table.

Title: Aluminum
Body HTML:
This humble metal used to be so difficult to refine that it was used as jewelry back in the 19th century until the Heroult process made it, well, dirt cheap! Today aluminum is second only to iron in industrial use and might even upstage the leader if it were only cheaper to make. And that day might come as the cost of electricity drops with new energy technology.

Our sample’s claim to fame is the heady grade of its purity: 99.996%!

Title: Gold
Body HTML:
Gold metal and inexpensive don't go together AT ALL of course but we try our best here at Luciteria.

This small container holds within it a gram of 99.95% pure gold in the form of a bar as pictured or ultr-pure granules. A beautiful if pricey addition to your elements collection!

Title: Bismuth
Body HTML:
Many an element collector started out by coming across these little souvenirs bought at museum gift stores or rock shops. The weird Escher-like formations happen when molten bismuth begins to solidify at an uneven pace along its surfaces. As the surfaces collide they change directions at right angles according to its lattice structure and in so doing create the odd-looking walls and stair steps. The iridescence is due to very thin layers of oxidation and are allowed to occur for purely aesthetic reasons; a little thicker here, a little thinner there in each case a different color is produced due to light refraction.

A beautiful crystal will be included in your container weighing between 5-10 grams.

Title: Copper
Body HTML:
Along with iron and gold, copper has been humanity's most well known and used metal and needs no introductory notes. About the only way to make this common metal more special is by purifying it to ridiculous levels. This small acrylic cube, which is meant to fit our medium periodic table case display as sold on our website, contains small nuggets of 99.9999% copper. Take moment to consider the engineering achievement of getting a metal so pure that only one atom in a million soils an otherwise perfect lattice of little Cu-Cu-Cus.

Looking like red peppers on a platter, these granules of copper will make for an especially colorful member of your collection.

Title: Cobalt
Body HTML:
As an alloying agent in steel to aid iron's otherwise modest attributes in hardness, and as a glass colorant, cobalt is little more than an industrial afterthought with the pure metal having few if any uses all on its own. Cobalt can be manufactured to a high degree of purity by an electrolytic process where it accumulates on the cathode ends from various solutions. From these cathodes the metal falls off in chunks and can then be machined into attractive shapes of various forms. Here's a little of this element from this process to grace your collection!

Photo shows a full container with about ten grams of cobalt metal.

Title: Gallium
Body HTML: In the last few years gallium has become the plaything of nerds of all ages thanks to increased availability via novelty shops and YouTube videos. Its lack of toxicity and relative low cost help things further, turning it into a safe alternative to mercury which has been all but banned. A fascinating characteristic of gallium, apart from its body temperature melting point, is that when it solidifies it expands like ice does so storing it in glass is inadvisable.

Title: Mercury
Body HTML:
Not too long ago it used to be the case that you could walk into any drugstore and pick a mercury thermometer off the rack for a couple of bucks. Many of these wound up being broken up on purpose by kids to play with the fun little metal balls that scurried to and fro. Times have changed and mercury has become synonymous with poison so a safety conscious world has seen to it that those thermometers share the same historical fate as shoe fitting X-ray scopes and cocaine tonics. But this reputation is in large part undeserved. True, mercury organometallics remain among the most feared of toxins and it's caused terrible environmental damage. Uncombined in its elemental state mercury is fairly inert which means it doesn't do much of anything.

You have the option here of taking a gram in an ampule as shown, recommended where children may have access to your collection, or loose metal drops in container for more savings.

Sorry, cannot be shipped internationally except as one gram samples. Domestic deliveries will be sent by UPS ground.

Title: Holmium
Body HTML:
This exotic metal is one of the fourteen other metals in the so-called Rare Earths group, none of which play any major role in industry outside of very specialized uses. Each one may have found its niche whether in lasers or as dopants with other elements to impart a desirable property. Holmium's claim to fame is as the Numero Uno among supermagnets beating out fellow Rare Earther neodymium for the title but much less popular due to it needing external activators.

A choice piece or two of holmium with its intriguing texture will make for a very unique collectible!

Title: Iodine
Body HTML:
For the collector of elements from the periodic table we find iodine is perhaps the most beautiful specimen as it comes in many attractive colors and forms. This element which we normally think of as a brown disinfectant can appear in various shades of amber, purple and magenta when dissolved and as black crystals in solid form. A sprinkling of pure iodine diffused in a non-polar solution becomes anywhere from light pink to deep purple while in a polar solution, such as in the gel offered here, turns a dramatic shade of amber.

Iodine is a tough element to place in a collection. Unless tightly sealed in a material that the element won’t dissolve (as iodine is very chemically reactive) it will soon stain everything around it. To make it behave we offer here tiny crystals trapped in glass. Opt for the cheaper solution with loose spheres but keep an eye on your sample that it’s not staining things nearby!

Title: Iridium
Body HTML: The title of rarest metal is a matter of controversy but iridium is always one of the top contenders. In addition to being super rare, iridium's extraordinary noble character finds uses in chemistry where an equally extraordinary resistance to corrosion is called for. And extraordinary also happens to be a good adjective to describe the fetching price of this handsome metal.

Title: Lutetium
Body HTML:
Lutetium is the most expensive element in the rare earths series. This is in part due to its rarity. A hundred pounds of monazite, its chief ore, are needed to get the piddly 2 grams of Lu in this ampule. But more than that it's the sheer difficulty of extricating it from its rare earth buddies with whom it's always found. And of all the REs lutetium is the most difficult to separate with there being literally hundreds of steps to get from the raw ore to the finished metal at a purity of 99.95%.

And we think it fitting to give 2 grams of lutetium in this offering the better to remind those holding it how much dirt had to be worked over to get this tiny amount that weighs about as much as a US dime!

Title: Magnesium
Body HTML:
Magnesium doesn't pose much of a challenge even if it's not something you can find at Home Depot. Widely used in industry as an alloying agent and also a lab staple for a wide variety of chemistry applications, it is most often sold as ribbons or powdered metal.

For this offer we’ve chosen an attractive and blindingly white piece of magnesium metal to fill the spot at number 12 on the ol’ table.

Title: Strontium
Body HTML:
This rarely seen metal is most typically associated with nuclear pollution. Finding the strontium-90 isotope in the atmosphere around your neighborhood is bad news indeed and should be cause for immediate plans to relocate to your mother-in-law's for a while. But don't worry, that should only happen if you live near a malfunctioning nuclear power plant.

Strontium's other isotopes are considerably less harmful. In fact, one big use of it goes into toothpaste because it coats teeth in a microscopic film that protects against cavities. The piece here would be enough to seal a city's worth of its denizen's teeth. We really don't recommend chewing on it though.

Title: Manganese
Body HTML:
An industrial metal, manganese is widely used to render desirable properties to steel such as strength and resistance to corrosion. However, it is much less commonly reduced to a pure state as it's also finicky to work with in this form. In particular, at high temperatures the metal is notoriously sensitive to oxidation. The very slightest trace of oxygen can spoil acres of its surface making for especially difficult procedures if one were to seek pristine surfaces.

Gleaming white manganese is hard to come by but we go out of our way to find the most attractive types :-)

Title: Nickel
Body HTML: We're all familiar with nickel because it's given its name to our 5-cent coin even though in that case it's really mostly copper. The name is better suited for Canadian nickels which were, in fact, made of 99.9% pure nickel for many years (but no longer). It is also always present in small amounts in ordinary steels and figures prominently in batteries and the manufacture of magnets. The sample here is of very high purity meant for use in the electronics industry where impurities become a much bigger issue.

Title: Osmium
Body HTML:
Osmium vies with equally exotic rhenium for rarest stable metal found in nature and with iridium for the title of heaviest. At only 1 part per billion in earth's crust and 75% heavier than lead osmium is something special indeed. While the world mines put out some 3,000 tons of gold every year all the osmium would be smaller than a basketball. To top it off osmium is a beautiful shade of light blue! Considering all these factors we suspect the day is not long in coming when someone very rich decides to make jewelry from it. Here's your chance to own a little of this wonderful metal encased in a little cube filled with gel. As you turn the cube the little osmium pieces slowly tumble and turn into new positions - fun!

This container will come with one gram of pure osmium metal.

Title: Phosphorus
Body HTML:
Phosphorus has been a staple of industry since the 1800's when it was found to be just the right ingredient for starting fires, thus beginning the phasing out of cumbersome flint-and-tinder methods of firestarting. However, in those early days it was the white, and not red, allotrope that was used. White phosphorus is not only self-igniting but also extremely toxic and following large public scandals the world slowly turned to the much less toxic red kind.

And to this day you see the brick red phsophorus on the side of matchbooks where it continues to have a presence in just about every home worldwide. Not too long ago phosphorus became mired in new controversy when a cheap process for making meth from cold medicine and phosphorus was revealed. While the phosphorus is in this case only a catalyst the DEA quickly put a stop to unregulated commerce in this chemical giving rise to the almost comical sight of junkies buying up matchbooks by the hundreds to scrape off the phosphorus.

Red phosphorus is a chemical with restrictions on its manufacture and distribution. To comply with these regulations the sample provided has been made inert through the addition of a resin binder. All sales will be recorded and made available to the DEA upon their request.

Alternately, consider the option of going with violet phosphorus. Although considerably more expensive, it is much rarer. The black allotrope is the rarest and little of it is available to anyone; by weight being more expensive than gold by a factor of 20!!

Title: Tellurium
Body HTML:
Tellurium is one of the rarest stable elements outside of the precious metals. Small amounts are used in electronics due to its unusual electrical properties…. but has few other uses leaving it in perennial obscurity.

For your collection we get you a beautiful chip of very, very pure tellurium. 99.99999% to be sure!

Title: Lead
Body HTML: Ok, let's be honest. Lead might be just a couple of protons away from gold but it's at the other end of the scale in desirability and its humility is reflected in its traditional uses. Fish sinkers, bullets and bathroom pipes. When setting out to complete a collection of all the elements you can "upgrade" to a higher level of purity than the commercial grade - and prices quickly go up to unrealistic levels when you reach into the 5- and 6N's - but here we offer only the “regular” type of lead rated at 99.9% purity at a price anyone can afford!

Title: Rhenium
Body HTML:
According to Wikipedia rhenium holds the title of rarest of the stable, naturally occurring metals with a concentration of only 0.7 parts per billion in the earth's crust. That's six times rarer than gold! This precious metal is very resistant to corrosion and has a very high melting point making it perfect for many applications.... if only it wasn't so rare and expensive! NASA and the Pentagon though have generous budgets which is why they can afford to use a large portion of all the tiny amount of rhenium mined every year to go into jet engines and rockets. Claim your little bit 'o the stuff for your collection before the Gov hogs up every last piece of it :-)

A one gram bead (or two half gram beads) of pure rhenium goes into each round container.

Title: Sulfur
Body HTML:
Sulfur. Vaguely hellish and certainly associated with all things hot and stinky, this powdery element can sometimes be bought in drug stores and is often typo'd as "sulphur". On rare occasion it is found in nature as a translucent yellow crystal but more often as powder stain near volcanoes. It is an essential ingredient in the making of rubber and is infamous for that awful smell of rotten eggs - which is actually the deadly gas hydrogen sulfide!

Much purer than the store-bought kind, this 99.99% sulfur has been specially prepared in a laboratory into beautiful small crystals that are translucent. Easily one of the most beautiful natural elements!

Title: Scandium
Body HTML: Scandium would be the perfect building metal if only it wasn't so damn rare! Light as aluminum but as strong as steel, scandium out-titaniums titanium as an exotic ingredient in high-tech doodads of the aerospace industry. China, Russia and Australia are the only countries where it is mined but of these only China currently refines it to its pure, metallic form. Our supplier, one of China’s leading exporters, boasts an annual production of less than 400 kilograms. That’s about the weight of a piano.

Title: Lithium
Body HTML:
Lithium hardly feels like metal when you hold it. I mean, it certainly looks like metal but you'd think you were holding popcorn. Not that it would stay looking metallic for long. Within seconds it turns black and in a few minutes after that start falling apart into a dusty mess. Make the mistake of tossing it in water and you'll see it catch on fire and make a hiss as if the damn thing's screaming at being on fire!

Here’s a little of this wonderful metal in a worry-free ampule that ensures the sample is kept beautifully shiny.

Title: Selenium
Body HTML:
Selenium is little known other than for its use in dandruff shampoo and as a vitamin supplement of dubious medical value... hardly a glamorous claim to fame. However obscure it may be it is very attractive in both its red and glassy black forms.

This is arguably this element’s most attractive and dramatic look. Glassy swirly things that are jet black and reflective as a mirror - irresistible! Hard to believe this is the same stuff whence the dull gray beads usually seen!

Title: Titanium
Body HTML: Titanium's growing popularity is due to excellent properties. Light, strong and corrosion resistant, titanium is the luxury version of steel. These days anything made of metal that wants to sound high end and high tech will sport titanium in the branding. From tiny screws to entire submarines have been made of titanium!

Title: Vanadium
Body HTML: Exotic-sounding vanadium's 15 minutes of fame never came being eternally tasked as an alloying agent in steel. Very seldom has any lab bothered to reduce the compounds it's normally found in to its native state but, and here's where that fame might be warranted, vanadium may well top the list of rarest element found in its native state when a few specks of the isolated metal were discovered on the side of a Mexican volcano. You go vanadium! In the meantime, here's a little fruit of that rare lab effort when someone took the trouble to see what the metal actually looks like in pure form. And so attractive too these little leaf-shaped sparkling crystals.

Title: Tungsten
Body HTML:
Of late tungsten is shaking off its obscurity (its biggest industrial use having been as light bulb filaments) and is gaining popularity as a cheaper alternative with which to make jewelry from. Because of its hardness when alloyed with carbon it is also the metal of choice with which to make cutting tools that resist wear much better than ordinary steel. And now its heavy weight (50% heavier than lead) has made chunks of it the playthings of gadget lovers.

For your collection, we’ll place about five grams’ worth of 99.95% pure and shiny tungsten rods.

Title: Cadmium
Body HTML:
Cadmium. They used to make rechargeable batteries from this stuff, remember? Its fifteen years of fame ended when lithium came to town and now this poor metal is back in obscurity.

Love it, hate it or be indifferent but here it is all the same: a chunk of cadmium in an all-transparent round plastic container to fill out your set!

Title: Thorium Dioxide
Body HTML:
Thorium Dioxide (or Thoria) is a substance that literally helped light up the world. While the great chemist Jöns Jacob Berzelius named the element after Thor, the Norse god of thunder, in 1829, it took a few more decades for it to become a household staple. In 1885, Carl Auer von Welsbach patented the "gas mantle," a silk mesh soaked in a mixture of 99% thorium dioxide and 1% cerium dioxide. When heated by a flame, the thoria would incandesce with a brilliant, steady white light that far outshone the flickering gas lamps of the era. It was a billion unit success that brought reliable light to the masses before the electric bulb took over.

But the story doesn't stop at lighting. Thoria is incredibly refractory, meaning it has one of the highest melting points of any oxide (3,300 degrees celcius!). This makes it indispensable for TIG welding electrodes and high-end camera lenses, where its high refractive index allows for thinner glass with less distortion. It even had a controversial stint as "Thorotrast," a medical X-ray contrast agent, before doctors realized that internalizing alpha-emitters was a bad idea (yikes!). Today, it’s the "holy grail" for fans of the Thorium Fuel Cycle, representing a future of safer, greener nuclear power. Whether you’re a fan of Victorian-era tech or 21st-century energy, this "history in a bottle" is a must-have trophy.

Title: Boron
Body HTML: Mysterious boron comes in two allotropes; a brown powder that looks rather identical to something you'd scrape off your shoe and a glassy type that looks like miniature asteroids. Despite being common, boron has a reputation for being fiendishly difficult to isolate into elemental form. That makes owning one of these specimens all the more special of course!

Title: Cerium
Body HTML:
The rare earths aren't really all that rare. While they're widely distributed all over the earth they're rare enough in the sense that there aren't that many locations where you can find them in concentrated deposits that make mining profitable. And when you do find those deposits the refining steps yield misch metal which is sort of an onomatopoeia for what it actually is: a mishmash of seventeen different metals. Getting those isolated from each other has been rated as one of the major chemical engineering headaches of metallurgical science! In any case, cerium will account for about half of the misch metal alloy and is the most abundant of those 17 elements.

Our cerium sample comes in a little argon-filled ampule to protect it from oxidation and weighs approximately one gram.

Title: Germanium
Body HTML:
Germanium is the only metal (or metalloid to be exact) that looks green. Just a slight tinge mind you, a yellow-green highlight when you look at it just right, but definitely there when you compare it side by side to your typical gray looking ordinary metal. It's a fetching sight to see a rock shaped object that is as reflective as the most well-polished mirror.

Here we offer pure germanium “rocks” in an attractive screw-top round container. The piece shown weighs five grams which nearly fills the container.

Title: Zirconium
Body HTML:
Another industrial metal, zirconium is especially resistant to corrosive fluids so is often found in fabrication processes where acids and fluid salts have to travel from one place to another. Think of a hot acid. Like a knife through butter when it comes to plastic or ordinary steel. But zirconium can stand up to it.

For this metal we include two squarish pieces making for a handsome presentation.

Title: Yttrium
Body HTML: This metal feels lonely in its assigned spot on the periodic table. It would rather be with its cousins down by the rare earths with which it's always associated in nature. For a collector, yttrium is considered a medium difficulty element to acquire. It is not expensive but it's not something you'd find at Walmart either.

Title: Calcium
Body HTML:
Even though you get it from drinking milk and yogurt people shouldn't be surprised to learn that calcium is a metal. After all, reading through the ingredients list on a cereal box there's iron and magnesium and others too. But the thing is that calcium isn't something most are familiar with as an object.

And yet, it really is a metal. On the moon it would make for a perfect metal to make, I don't know, a cell tower or something. It's very light and plentiful. Why the moon? Well, because there's no atmosphere or water there that could come in contact with our imaginary calcium tower. Either of those touch it and the calcium instantly sizzles and corrodes.

So keeping it in metallic form here on earth takes a little work. We've put this piece in a glass ampule with argon gass to do the trick.

Title: Molybdenum
Body HTML:
Molybdenum may not be well known in the collective mind of the public but figures prominently in the world of metallurgy as one of the ingredients that, together with iron, makes steel. "Moly steel" is a collective term used for many steel grades containing varying amounts of molybdenum all of which yield much higher strength than iron alone. In metallurgical terms strength is that property that allows the object to retain its shape under a load; ie. a steel plate not buckling when a weight has been placed on it.

Here we have about 5 grams of pure molybdenum for your collection.

Title: Indium
Body HTML:
Germanium is named after Germany and francium after France but indium is not named after India. The name was instead inspired from spectroscopy where its discoverer analyzed a sample containing this metal gave off an indigo glow, a beautiful color halfway between blue and purple.

Indium is quite rare and expensive and is unusual for being so soft that you can knead it with your fingers!

Title: Potassium
Body HTML:
Potassium is another element whose name is familiar to most but few have actually seen. When great care is taken to separate a very pure sample of potassium it appears as shiny as the silver backing of a mirror. For that level of conservation you would need to store it under vacuum or an inert gas because otherwise it would very quickly oxidize. Even when stored in inert mineral oil traces of contaminants are enough to spoil the surface finish and turn the metal whitish purple (which is kind of attractive as far as corrosion goes!)

Potassium is also very light and will float on water. Except doing so would be foolish, least of all because it would ruin your precious sample! A metal chunk thrown in water will immediately catch on fire and then explode releasing toxic but admittedly quite colorful byproducts!

Here is a tiny lab-made ampule containing an estimated 100mg of this metal absolutely free of unisghtly blemishes caused by oxidation.

Title: Tantalum
Body HTML:
This exotic metal is named after the Greek god Tantalus who was tormented for all eternity to stand in water and with hanging fruit all around yet was unable to quench either thirst or hunger as the object of his torment kept them just outside of reach. The element, too, is outside of the reach of most. Despite being incredibly dense no hardware store stocks tantalum hammers. It is among the most corrosion resistant metals known but there's practically nothing available taking advantage of this property. Why? Because it's so rare and expensive.

Today the world has an insatiable appetite for cell phones and other electronics where tantalum has been found to be an ideal metal for capacitors and other doodads. What little of it is mined every year happens to be worth a lot of money. Sadly, its principal mines happen to be smack-dab in the middle of the Congo and Rwanda where ownership of these mines is incessantly tussled over by AK-47 wielding thugs right alongside other commodities like diamonds and ivory.

Anyway, here's your chance to own a little of this metal in the form of thick foil that has been snipped into squarish pieces adding up to a gram or two. Purity is over 99.99%.

Title: Zinc
Body HTML:
Whenever you see the word "galvanized" in relation to anything metal that's zinc they're talking about. Galvanizing (coating in zinc) is done to give an oxidation-prone metal a little more resistance to the elements. Which is ironic because zinc is a rather fragile metal in said elements. Pure zinc is a dazzling metal with a very faint blue-green hue. That beautiful luster slowly dulls in air until it's all gone and the zinc has turned a dirty looking gray.

A small piece weird-shaped piece similar to the one pictured will come with your container and, being airtight, should keep it shiny for the long term!

Title: Cesium
Body HTML:
This rare metal is much sought after by collectors. While not especially dangerous in small amounts, the major danger being skin contact burns, the lack of industrial uses and its natural rarity mean a very meager supply.

We’re happy to stock these small ampules specially made for Luciteria. Each comes with approximately 100mg of 99.99% pure cesium metal. And yes, you can melt it in your hand and watch it flow from one side to the other :-)

Title: Niobium
Body HTML: Niobium is one of the more exotic metals to figure prominently in jewelry. While it's certainly rare and pricey enough to merit being sold as jewelry the main attraction is actually its ability to form very thin films of many different colors. Anodization, the process by which a metal is selectively oxidized at an anode in an electrolytic bath, goes hand in hand with niobium destined for jewelry. Niobium oxide has the ability to diffract light at specific wavelengths depending on the thickness. The thinnest layers, measuring only a few atoms across, will impart a brown tinge and as the thickness increases it goes through blue, purple, red and green with many shades in between. So here's a perfect chance to liven up your set with a little of this handsome metal!

Title: Barium
Body HTML:
Barium is one of the rarer metals to acquire for the collector. The main problem stems from its very reactive nature that makes it very difficult to keep it from oxidizing. The difficulty in reducing the metal from the ores as well as the lack of a substantial market for barium in elemental form make difficult to acquire and expensive.

As is the case with the rest of the reactive metals on offer in these acrylic boxes, we embed the samples in a little vial immune to the ravages of air’s oxygen!

Title: Samarium
Body HTML: On the lanthanides row of the periodic table samarium is the first metal that doesn't rust in plain old air. It also happens to be one of the most of the series though that hardly means you can buy cutlery made of the stuff. In fact, about the only thing where samarium is widely used is as flints in cigarette lighters. This exploits one of the traits of the rare earths which is that they create showers of sparks when struck by another metal as small, overheated particles rapidly oxidize.

Title: Silver
Body HTML: Ah, silver. Poor man's gold. A volcano near Potosi, Bolivia was so full of silver ore that it is said the Spaniards literally carried away a substantial chunk of the mountain! Today silver fever is still going strong and the metal's annual output from mining and recycling is eagerly claimed by investors and jewelers alike. Time for you to get in on the action and claim your own little bit!

Title: Rhodium
Body HTML: Rhodium is among the rarest of metals and, being a member of the platinum group, it is also growing in popularity in the world of bullion investment and jewelry. In the cheaper tier of jewelry making it is associated with chemically applied thin plating to go over cheaper alloys underneath both to protect it from wear and corrosion as well as to give it a more upscale look. High end jewelry has been designed in solid rhodium to outrageous prices but despite demand the output has been small due to the considerable technical difficulties in working with this metal as it is brittle and requires very high temperatures. Most commercially available rhodium is hot pressed sintered powder which comes close, but does not quite achieve, the full density of the metal.

Title: Thulium
Body HTML:
This obscure metal is little more than a lab curiosity. Rare, expensive and difficult to separate from the other lanthanides, along with the fact it has few extraordinary properties, means thulium is destined to remain in obscurity. As the number of collectors of elements grow we wonder how long it will be before the majority of the demand simply comes from those wanting to own a little of this exotic metal!

These fragments of thulium come in the wonderfully textured form of the metal known in the trade as "dendritic" which is a fancy word for the branch-like shapes metals form when allowed to crystallize.

Title: Uranium
Body HTML:
Nature's alphabet ends with the letter U. The Strong Nuclear Force, the far less well known sister to gravity and electromagnetism, is responsible for sheepherding protons and neutrons into tidy family units. But the SNF's mojo can only carry things so far. Once a family grows too big things can get ugly and relations can break down. Explosively so. There is no magic number but in the case of 235, well, that happens to be an especially troublesome number of family members corraled into one condo which we'll call an "atom". You see, when a dispute breaks out in Uranium clan 235 the violence can break out into neighboring atoms and all hell breaks loose.

Oddly, if a couple more cousins show up things aren't so bad. Uranium-238, otherwise known as depleted uranium or "DU", is of the biggest "pretty stable" atoms that strong nuclear force can manage. That means the metal is actually quite low in radioactivity, relatively speaking, and the type of radioactivity it emits is composed of a comparatively heavy 4-man team composed of two protons and two neutrons. In essence, when uranium breaks apart it farts high-speed helium. And that just doesn't hurt much.

A little more seriously, that's not to say DU is harmless. If allowed to turn into airborne dust it will lodge in your lungs and pepper your alveoli like a machine gun with infinite bullets. While they may not hurt much at any one moment the cumulative effect over years can wreck DNA and degrade tissues into cancerous tumors. Not good.

We offer small chips of DU as shown (totalling one gram in this offer). The U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission allows for the sale and transportation of uranium to the public if certain conditions are met; the most pressing of which deals with the maximum allowable quantity any one person may own (1.5kg) and that it may not be exported outside of the country. For full details please see NRC §40.22 If you can live with these limitations then you should definitely consider owning a little of this marvel of nature.

As stated above, we are unable to ship uranium internationally (sorry).

Title: Silicon
Body HTML:
Silicon is one of the most common elements found on earth. Sand, for one, is nothing but rusted silicon. You can't find it in its elemental form in nature because at the heated temperatures found in the primordial earth all of it oxidized or bound to other elements. Yet, when it is purified it is fairly inert and will stay that way even in moist, salty air.

Our silicon comes from the large rods, or boules, that get sliced up into wafers to be etched for microchips. At 99.9999999% pure it is the purest grade of any element that is commercially available. These chips are a gorgeous, reflective dark blue-gray and we're sure you'll love your little sample!

Title: Palladium
Body HTML: Palladium is coming into its own as a (slightly) cheaper alternative to gold and platinum jewelry. Demand is growing also because it is increasingly melted into ingots for bullion investors and the automotive industry which utilizes it in catalytic converters.

Title: Hydrogen
Body HTML:
The universe itself could be a synonym for hydrogen considering it alone represents over 90% of all matter. Yet here on earth the gas is extremely rare except where it's chemically bound to something else (like, of course, water). That is because hydrogen is so light that it floats up into the upper atmosphere and beyond to outer space.

Comes in two varieties: regular and a special, low pressure gas ampule which will glow when ionized under high voltage. See colors at bottom of elements page

Trapped in one of these little ampules, inside a box with gel, the little gas here is going nowhere and will grace your collection for a long time!

Comes as a low pressure gas ampule which will glow when ionized under high voltage. See colors at bottom of elements page

Title: Neon
Body HTML:
Made famous by orange signs a century ago, little of the gas is actually employed today in lighting having been replaced instead by colored fluorescent lighting and, more recently, by LEDs. Still, say neon sign and everyone will think of a shop sign with words made of lit up glass tubing. You can, to be sure, still find the old fashioned neon signs for a (pricey) retro look that no cheap LED can replicate.

This gas ampule containing 99.9% pure neon gas will glow when ionized under high voltage. See colors at bottom of elements page

Title: Fluorine gas
Body HTML:
Fluorine is among the most viciously corrosive elements known to man. It is unique in that it can attack glass which is famous for, among other things, being one of the least reactive of materials. When you see a commercial sign with a frosted logo etched in glass you're seeing the handiwork of a stencil upon whose openings was unleashed fluorine.

This, naturally, presents a problem for those who want to obtain this gas for a collection. Fluorine normally is carried in specially treated steel flasks - which is not exactly ideal for showing off. So how does one get fluorine to behave itself and not attack its glassy enclosure? Well, it has been found that diluted down in two parts out of three with nitrogen that that is sufficiently weakened to prevent it from reacting with the glass. Sure, you're only getting your fluorine "watered down" but at least it's the genuine article on this exceptionally difficult and expensive element to score.

Fluorine has a slight yellow-green hue but it’s only evident when you have a lot of it concentrated in one place. Due however to the small size of the glass containers the gas inside appears colorless.

In its elemental form, fluorine is extremely difficult to find for the average consumer because it’s such a wildly dangerous substance. Their small size here limits the danger to a bad fart if one were to break (which is a good thing considering the alternative!) but just in case should be treated with care regardless.

Please note that unlike the other gases sold on this page fluorine doesn’t glow when excited by an electrical current. This is due to its being mixed with nitrogen and sealed at ambient pressure.

CAS 7782-41-4

Title: Oxygen
Body HTML:
It's hard to think of anything crucial to us human beings that doesn't involve oxygen. Not just the air we breathe and the water we drink but we find it in literally every protein that makes up our bodies and every bite we eat. Oxygen is life itself!

A little ampule containing a little of this life-giving O2 in pure form.

Comes in two varieties: regular and a special, low pressure gas ampule which will glow when ionized under high voltage. See colors at bottom of elements page

Title: Nitrogen
Body HTML:
If you were to judge solely by YouTube videos, nitrogen's only use is to freeze things so hard that they shatter. The same can be done with any other elemental gas, of course, but nitrogen is the cheapest to liquefy. Thanks also to its relative inertness it is a staple of every laboratory.

Here you have a little of it at a perfectly safe room temperature :-)

You get one pure nitrogen gas ampule which will glow when ionized under high voltage. See colors at bottom of elements page

Title: Helium
Body HTML:
Almost every kid who's had a birthday party can relate to the fun of sucking on a helium balloon to hear yourself talk at an unnaturally high pitch. This happens because your vocal cords resonate at a frequency dependent on the speed of sound in a medium. Helium, being lighter than air, is less dense and allows a correspondingly higher speed of sound transmission. The compression of the sound waves therefore raises the pitch.

No, the little bit of helium in this ampule will not make the box float in air.

Your helium round will come with a gas ampule filled with helium gas which will glow when ionized under high voltage. See colors at bottom of elements page

Title: Krypton
Body HTML:
It is true that krypton gas, as well as the rest of the noble gases, is technically free for the taking. The atmosphere has a near-limitless supply of it. However, extracting it most certainly isn't free or even cheap. At only 1 part in a million, a laboratory seeking to get, say, a couple liters' worth of krypton would have to liquefy enough air to fill an Olympic size swimming pool! Just imagine the cost of electricity alone for that task.

Krypton's bright glow lends itself to a number of lighting applications but the small ampule here has as its purpose to sit in your collection and look pretty :-)

Comes as a low pressure gas ampule which will glow when ionized under high voltage. See colors at bottom of elements page

Title: Bromine
Body HTML:
Some people are unlucky enough to live in overly hot climes with un-air-conditioned rooms so stuffy that gallium and even cesium remain in their liquid state but, generally speaking, there are only two elements that are liquid at "room temperature"; mercury and bromine. 

Liquids tend to evaporate and bromine is especially difficult to contain. No mere screwtop jar could keep it bottled up. Even not counting its very corrosive effect eating its way out of the lid this element is a notorious "escape artist" which will exploit the tiniest crack or pore to let itself out of that prison and roam out into the air freely. And that's really a bad idea, you see, because it is extremely irritating to the lungs - so much so, in fact, that it's used for making tear gas!

For this reason bromine is one of the most toxic chemicals found in their elemental state. While the quantity offered here for collectors is very small nevertheless a strong disclaimer applies that it should be handled with care and to take all necessary precautions to avoid breaking the ampule within accidentally or otherwise.

Title: Praseodymium
Body HTML:
When a talk show host wants to drive home the point that there are some very rare metals in your cell phone he might be tempted to give praseodymium as the leading example. After all, it appears nearly unpronounceable and would be familiar only to those who work with rare earth metals!

In actuality this timid metal isn't all that rare. The dirt you walk on has plenty. But, and there's always a but, getting the metal isolated from its earthy prison is not a trivial task. Also, keeping it from turning right back into "earth" is equally difficult. This is why it's offered here inside of a little glass vial which protects it from coming into contact with air or other things that prevent it from, you know, turning into that dirt we speak of!

Title: Lanthanum
Body HTML:
Lanthanum gives its name to a whole row on the period table, the lanthanides. Apart from this the little-known element is famous for little else. The metal is soft and rapidly corrodes if left out in the open.

The specimen provided here was carefully prepared in an all-glass enclosure with argon to keep it pure and bright.

Title: Rubidium
Body HTML:
Rubidium is a rare and expensive metal with few uses. Ironically, its rarity in part is due to there being few recipes that mark Rb as an important ingredient. It have a solitary extra electron in its outer shell means it will hungrily pair up with almost any other element and form an especially strong bond. This explains why it is expensive on top of being rare: a number of tiresome processes are required to break that chemical bond and leave the metal isolated in its pure state.

The collector will note that rubidium is probably the most difficult of the naturally occurring and stable elements to acquire. And gram for gram it is much more expensive than gold - at least as prepared for lab use such as in this tiny ampule containing about 100 milligrams. 

Very shiny and handsome, the element tends toward an off-white creaminess and is extremely soft... a characteristic not immediately apparent since freeing it from the glass will immediately invite a violent reaction as the metal quickly grabs on to just about any material it comes into contact with including, of course, the oxygen in the air.

Title: Ruthenium
Body HTML: This little-known member of the precious metals family is very rare in nature. Found in Russia, and named after this country, ruthenium is associated with platinum ores along which it’s always found in trace amounts. And after someone takes the trouble to isolate it jewelers mix right back in with platinum to make the alloy harder. Because it is a precious metal there is growing interest among investors in buying it as bullion.

Title: Sodium
Body HTML:
Soft enough to be parted with a butter knife, sodium is a substance that few are familiar with in its metal form. Salt though is synonymous with sodium. Simultaneously a dietary necessity and a constant cautionary against overconsumption lest you put your heart in dire trouble.

But sodium is everywhere and its uses too numerous to list. What is certain, however, is that having a little of it bright ‘n shiny is a special thing indeed!

Title: Full Sets with Display Case
Body HTML:
For the science nerd in your family few gifts can match the utter coolness of owning a periodic table not as an ugly wall chart but as the real thing with each slot taken up by a pure piece of that element. And where these sets have in the past cost thousands and come in ugly cases Luciteria wants your collection to be both affordable and beautiful! While it can be wall mounted with additional support - not included - this was designed to lay flat as a coffee table display where gravity doesn’t pull the samples out of the way (or onto the floor!). It measures 25.5"x15.5" (and about an inch thick) or 64x39x3cm

We start with a periodic table in acrylic colorfully yet elegantly printed with each element’s name symbol and atomic number above which sits a cutout ready to hold an alltransparent screwtop container. Buying a plain case therefore allows you the cheapest entry into this fascinating hobby. Many elements in their pure or nearly pure form are easily accessible and joining the hunt is as fun as it is educational.

We offer also the option of buying sets that are pre-filled with beautiful, high-grade samples ready to go on display at a very reasonable cost.

The basic set is the first and most affordable of these. It comes with the case and 47 different elements each being of 99% purity or higher. This set costs over a thousand dollars if bought separately but buy it as a bundle and pay only $495 plus shipping!

The full set includes all 85 elements that are collectible including the commercially rare radioactive samples, elements that must be stored under inert gas to prevent them from oxidizing and small samples of the pricey platinum metals group! Fit for a 21st century science classroom or the kind of work office meant to impress!

Title: Platinum
Body HTML:
Platinum is a metal that can’t get out from under gold’s shadow. Despite it often being worth more, and certainly marketed as a fancier alternative, the yellow colored metal remains the yardstick upon which every platinum comparison hinges.

But spill no tears for good ole No. 78 as the atom is unlikely to sign up for therapy sessions (heh). This dense, hue-less metal possesses the additional enviable properties of being impervious to even the strongest acids and pretty much the strongest flames too!

Here’s a one gram bar of platinum beautifully stamped with the assayer’s logo and purity assessment.

Title: Arsenic
Body HTML:
This infamous element has the dubious credit of being the tool by which who knows how many people were murdered. While the pure element as shown isn’t highly toxic its oxides sure are. A little bit in food goes unnoticed and over the course of days or weeks the victim’s health declines until they have just enough strength to sign over a last will and testament to the merriment of the poisoner. It was a death that back in the old day, before blood lab tests and autopsies, left few clues as to what did in the poor sap.

Once autopsies and blood tests came into use during the late 19th century and into the 20th arsenic’s role in killing people gave way to its use as a poison of vermin but this, too, was eventually phased out in favor of other chemicals. The 21st century has resuscitated arsenic for use in the making of gallium arsenide transistors.

It is this last for which high purity arsenic is refined today in electronics laboratories. And for you we’ll pick a big, beautiful crystal for your collection. Well, if you have a liberal interpretation of beauty at least.

Title: Europium
Body HTML:
Together with lutetium, europium is a very rare element - perhaps not in the absolute sense as it is comparatively abundant but certainly in the commercial sense where far fewer kilograms of europium come on the market any given year than truly rare metals like gold or platinum.

Much of the reason for this is that europium quickly oxidizes in air turning it from a metal into, well, an earthy type of material of little obvious use. I say “obvious” because this metal still does have plenty of uses; in electronics particularly.

In this offering we keep it lustrous with the aid of inert argon gas which will not chemically react with this or any other metal.

Title: Xenon
Body HTML:
Go out for a night drive and most cars’ headlights are kind of yellow but a few luxury models will have a dazzling blue-white light. That’s the color of xenon gas as it glows when an ungodly amount of heat, pressure and electricity come together in its presence.

This heavy (for a gas) element has another cool trick up its sleeve. Inside a pressurized ampule such as this one it will turn into a liquid when chilled below 16°C (50°F). Hold it in your hands for few seconds as it heats back up past this point and the liquid inside vanishes before your eyes back to its gaseous state! To save money you can also opt for the normal non-pressurized type which when cooled does…. nothing. Oh well, still a neat addition :-)

Title: Hafnium
Body HTML: This is hafnium, an obscure and costly metal few have heard of and one man has yet to have much of a use for outside of very limited specialty uses. Hard, dense and highly resistant to corrosion it is a metal well suited to be fashioned into jewelry or bullion someday when the world wakes up to just how precious a commodity it really is.

Title: Empty 31mm round plastic container
Body HTML: For… in case you need some empty ones!

Title: Argon
Body HTML:
Few people have heard of this gaseous element even though it makes up a sizeable portion of the atmosphere. Well, 1% to be sure but that’s still much more common than much more familiar gases like hydrogen or helium.

Because of its electron shell configuration, argon is unable to form chemical bonds, and thus molecules, with any other element except perhaps under extreme conditions never found in nature. This characteristic is exploited commercially whenever there arises the need to protect any type of chemicals from oxidizing. This being a gas, of course, limits the possibilities to only those situations where argon and the material to be protected can be contained. In this capacity it features prominently throughout Luciteria’s ampule offerings where except for the sale of other gas elements any reactive ampule is filled with argon to keep the sample from reacting with anything else.

Title: Tellurium metal 99.999%
Body HTML:
More fragile than glass, tellurium is a very rare element found together with copper. Copper, too, happens to be rare in nature as well but - luckily - what little there is tends to bulk up together making recovery easy. In mining copper tellurium is recovered as a valuable impurity and due to its halfway-metal, halfway-something-else it has unique electrical properties that can be exploited. More uses have been employing tellurium and its growth in industry is really only checked by its rarity.

CAS 13494-80-9

Title: Carbon
Body HTML: Carbon is so interesting. It is found in its humble form of coal and its coveted form of diamonds. Allotropes present the collector of elements with a choice as they have to pick one or the other or shoehorn two or more types into the slot. In the "humble" form carbon has several further sub-choices and you can pick any color as long as it's black! Amorphous, crystalline, glassy and nanotubes can all be selected as representing the sixth element on the periodic table.

Title: Samarium metal 99.9%
Body HTML:
As far as collecting the elements go the only thing that is remarkable about samarium is that it's the cheapest of the fifteen in the rare earths group to acquire. Its one major application is for making strong magnets when alloyed with cobalt. We stock pure samarium in the form known as “dendrites” which exhibit a thoroughly fascinating bark-like texture and is also a mark of their high level of purity.

Note that when freshly made samarium is a dazzling white as pictured but over time darkens a bit due to tarnishing. However, the metal retains its luster.

Note additional options for this element on the Ampules section

CAS 7440-19-9

Title: Platinum
Body HTML:
Platinum is a metal that can’t get out from under gold’s shadow. Despite it often being worth more, and certainly marketed as a fancier alternative, the yellow colored metal remains the yardstick upon which every platinum comparison hinges.

But spill no tears for good ole No. 78 as the atom is unlikely to sign up for therapy sessions (heh). This dense, hue-less metal possesses the additional enviable properties of being impervious to even the strongest acids and pretty much the strongest flames too!

Here’s a one gram bar of platinum beautifully stamped with the assayer’s logo and purity assessment.

Title: Scandium metal 99.9%
Body HTML:
Prized by a handful of collectors worldwide, scandium has precious few uses because of its extreme rarity and high cost of manufacture. Small amounts are alloyed into aluminum to improve strength, particularly at weld joints.

Annual scandium metal production is estimated to be less than five tons worldwide - the weight of your average truck! So owning a gram or two actually makes you a significant shareholder in the exotic metals leaderboard. Now how cool is that?? :-)

CAS 7440-20-2

Title: Titanium metal 99.95% "eggs"
Body HTML:
Easter candy? Nope. We had these egg-shaped pieces specially made for us to liven up element collections suffering from a little too much gray! This is not commercial grade titanium as used in jewelry, which is normally alloyed with aluminum and other metals, but instead 99.95% lab-grade purity. They were then individually anodized in intense, transluscent colors. Anodization is not paint or dye. It is a patina that is part of the metal's surface and will not wash off or discolor.

Each "egg" measures 25mm in length and weighs 16.9 grams.

Title: Fluorine gas
Body HTML:
Fluorine is among the most viciously corrosive elements known to man. It is unique in that it can attack glass which is famous for, among other things, being one of the least reactive of materials. When you see a commercial sign with a frosted logo etched in glass you're seeing the handiwork of a stencil upon whose openings was unleashed fluorine.

This, naturally, presents a problem for those who want to obtain this gas for a collection. Fluorine normally is carried in specially treated steel flasks - which is not exactly ideal for showing off. So how does one get fluorine to behave itself and not attack its glassy enclosure? Well, it has been found that diluted down in two parts out of three with nitrogen that that is sufficiently weakened to prevent it from reacting with the glass. Sure, you're only getting your fluorine "watered down" but at least it's the genuine article on this exceptionally difficult and expensive element to score.

Fluorine has a slight yellow-green hue but it’s only evident when you have a lot of it concentrated in one place. Due however to the small size of the glass containers the gas inside appears colorless.

In its elemental form, fluorine is extremely difficult to find for the average consumer because it’s such a wildly dangerous substance. Their small size here limits the danger to a bad fart if one were to break (which is a good thing considering the alternative!) but just in case should be treated with care regardless.

Please note that unlike the other gases sold on this page fluorine doesn’t glow when excited by an electrical current. This is due to its being mixed with nitrogen and sealed at ambient pressure.

CAS 7782-41-4

Title: "One of Each" Set
Body HTML:
If you’re looking to start a collection of the elements from the periodic table here’s your shortcut to get them all in one shot - and at a discount. On offer here are sets built around a sample of every possible element ready to be displayed in handy little screwtop bottles each with its own label.

The centerpiece is our new custom acrylic display case with cutouts to fit these bottles. Each case measures 14” x 8.5” (your average laptop) and consists of two acrylic panels separated by metal standoffs to create a very professional look. The case itself is fully labeled and color coded to distinguish natural vs. radioactive elements… it’s just waiting to be filled! Buy the display case all by itself and start the chase with pure elements you can find right at home!

For convenience and savings we also offer groups of elements in the same 3ml, labeled bottles.

We start with the blue set which comprises all the basic elements from the periodic table that are relatively easy to find and are stable in air. You get a total of 46 highly purified 1-gram samples of each in a labeled glass vial (see diagram on this page for full list).

Next up is the red set; the more exotic elements. This 35pc set includes glass ampules inside bottles to maintain the metallic nature of air sensitive element samples. Except for the gases these all contain an approximate net weight of 100mg.

The purple set includes four radioactive elements which are all extremely difficult to find offered for sale anywhere. Don’t worry about their safety. Because of the small size samples their level of radioactivity is far too small to be hazardous to health so long as you keep these well out of the reach of children. Accidental ingestion, or particulates which are allowed to become airborne where they could be breathed in, do pose a serious health risk. This set includes radium (as radium sulfate trace on a vintage watch dial), radon and thorium (both as thorium dioxide, 100mg) and one gram of uranium metal.

Collect them piecemeal little by little or go for the full set from day one which includes all 85 collectible elements, as well as the display case.

Please note that uranium and thorium samples may not ship internationally.

NOTE: Current export restrictions from China are preventing us from selling complete sets. Please inquire for more information.

Title: Purple Gold
Body HTML:
What sorcery is this, a purple metal? It takes a lot for us to carry something that isn’t a pure element since this goes contrary to our mission but we’re making an exception our customers are likely to forgive. When chemistry turns the geek factor dial to 10 they wind up with unholy concoctions like this. In nature gold is famous for alloying with basically absolutely nothing. Inertness is that property characteristic of noble metals and gases alike that shun the company of other elements which is the reason gold does not oxidize even in the most adverse conditions.

Well, unless someone decides to mix it with aluminum, that is. A physicist once theorized that a synthetic ratio of the two metals should yield an unnatural hue of purple and eventually a chemist set out to find out whether it was true. It was. This vivid color is not some Photoshop trick and while it may look like a colored candy wrapper each small brittle piece cleaves to show an equally purple interior that is unique in the whole of metallurgy.

If it wasn’t for this brittleness the jewelry industry would have long ago pounced on this beautiful creation to add alongside the more trite gold alloy variants of “white” and “pink” and so on. But that’s ok. The fact that you can’t find purple gold rings and pendants and whatnot in every mall makes this all the more special.

Title: Sulfur crystals 99.99%
Body HTML:
As available for industry, sulfur is an opaque solid. Allowed to cool, the deep yellow liquid briefly solidifies into a translucent solid that turns hazy within minutes. If, however, the cooling process is precisely controlled macroscopic crystals form that maintain their translucency. Masses of see-through sulfur crystals can also sometimes be found in nature which are coveted by mineral collectors.

While no purer than the standard variety, these lab-created crystals are without question a visual upgrade for only a modest cost premium.

Title: Zirconium crystal 99.9%
Body HTML:
When grown in ultrapure form using the sexily named Arkel-Boer process(!) zirconium sports this really cool reptilian-looking skin. The need for ultrapure zirconium is dictated primarily by the nuclear industry which forms it into tubes meant to house the uranium or plutonium reactor fuel at the heart of the reactor.

We intercept a small amount of this otherwise nuke-bound zirconium for the benefit of appreaciative collectors :-)

Title: Blue Gold
Body HTML:
We’re cautiously violating our “only pure elements” motto again with the addition of this exotic alloy. Unlike the way better known white and rose golds of the jewelry world, the black gold of the industrial world (which sounds so much more poetic than “crude oil”) and like its true counterpart purple gold, blue gold is a mixture of gold and indium that results in a bluish concoction that looks nothing like the color or texture of either of its constituents. In fact, it closely approximates crystalline osmium though, of course, much less dense.

Just as the case with purple gold, blue gold is brittle and crumbles easily meaning that it’s basically unsuitable to do anything with it save for some delicate castings. They are both products - inventions really - born of bored scientists on downtime who had nothing better to do than see what happens when you mix and match different metals, I think ;-)

Title: Titanium Black 99.9%
Body HTML:
Titanium is normally a gray metal with a very light brown tint evident only when placed right next to a flat, fully colorless gray metal like platinum. When anodized it can take on the colors of the rainbow thanks to the prismatic effects of extremely thin layers of oxidation. And then there is a third color that is virtually unknown in metallurgy: pure black.

Fully blackened metals do not exist in nature without the help of oxides or the chemical combination of other elements. Titaium is no different, technically, but the effect can be simulated through microporosity. Light that strikes specially treated surfaces of pure titanium is caught and absorbed in a similar fashion to ‘Vantablack’ and other similar carbon concoctions famous for being, well, so utterly black. Without the light coming back to your eyes your bran interprets it as basically a little slice of night.

Titanium in and of itself can’t pull the same level of creepy blackness but these ovals all the same look as though they were made from a stony un-metallic material that is betrayed the second you hold them in your hands! Each one measures about an inch in length and weighs some 15-20 grams.

Title: Deuterium Oxide 99.9% (Heavy Water)
Body HTML:
Few are aware that 1 out of every 6420 water molecules found in nature, or ~0.00016%, is abnormal; the H in that H2O molecule is stuck to a neutron. This is known as deuterium and when oxidized it’s named deuterated water. It is more commonly referred to as “heavy water” because that extra neutron adds some 10% more mass to the molecule.

Heavy water is more than just a scientific curiosity. Millions of gallons of water are harvested with the intention of getting at that 0.00016% as it is useful in nuclear reactors as a means to aid the fission process central to the power generation of nuclear reactors. It’s also key in the production of plutonium. For this reason heavy water is associated with nuclear power in the mind of the public and to some it carries the whiff of radioactivity or at least industrial pollution. However, it is a natural product that is integral to every living being. Evolution has co-opted this rare little molecule and put it to use where it serves as a regulator in cellular respiration. Too much or too little throws that balance out of whack with negative health effects.

So what would happen if you drank this potion? Nothing really. Substitute all your water intake with nothing but heavy water though and it won’t be long before you’re in the ICU. The perfect poison then? Nope, not that either. The prospective murderer would need to get several gallons of the stuff to do the deed… at a cost of tens of thousands of dollars. And since its main producers are India and Iran that would present some interesting logistical problems of their own!

Anyway, as a collectible your ampule of heavy water has at least one neat feature: it freezes at 4°C (39°F) which means that after freezing it will stay in your fridge as unmeltable ice indefinitely! Because of the effect of supercooling this is best done by putting it in the freezer rather than simply lowering the temp to 4°C. Note, however, that to avoid the risk of shattering the glass (from the expansion of the ice) you should place it laying down rather than standing up.

15X35mm glass ampule. CAS 7789-20-0

International customers note that this product may not be allowed for import. As of November 2022 we have completed over 100 international deliveries without a problem but, to avoid the risk of your order being returned or confiscated, please research beforehand if it’s allowed into your country.

Title: Thorium Chromate
Body HTML:
Thorium is the most difficult to source element Luciteria carries. In fact, unlike any of our other items and despite its high cost what little we manage to get our hands on typically sells out quickly, leaving it more or less in a permanent “sold out” state.

The poor man’s alternative, for the collector at least, has been to get a sample of the much more easily found thorium dioxide or specialty welding rods that have a small percentage of thorium alloyed in. To ease the long wait and punch to the wallet when it comes to getting pure metallic thorium we now add this colorful stand-in which is both plentiful and affordable.

So, wait, you might wonder, why not take this powder and use it to make thorium metal? It’s not like it hadn’t occurred to us. The chemistry of thorium is such that it’s quite difficult to separate it from oxygen. Normally, a lab presented with this problem simply reaches for another element that is “hungrier” for oxygen as a sacrificial lamb leaving the target nice and shiny and clean all over.

On the surface, this should also be possible with thorium which is less reactive chemically than something like cesium or barium - two elements that are remarkably sensitive to oxygen - but there is the added dimension here that the necessary reactions have to take place at a temperature sufficiently high to melt these chemicals. And here’s the critical problem because thorium’s melting point is so high that ordinary furnaces can’t melt it. On top of that there’s the issue of radioactivity which means that even if a lab already has one of these specialty furnaces they’re going to be very reluctant to risk tainting it for other work. Bottom line: very few have the ability to get the job done.

Finally, there’s the cost of metallic thorium. Even tiny amounts are often beyond the budget of many collectors. For this reason, compounds like this thorium chromate make for a good alternative.

When measured directly by a dosimeter, each ampule emits approximately 0.75 μSv/hr. The tube that it ships in cuts this down to 0.4μSv. For comparison, note that average background radiation is generally around 0.1-0.4μSv/hr (1.5-3mSv/yr).

For our international customers: note that radioactive materials might be regulated in your country. Please check with your postal laws prior to ordering this item.

Title: Yellowcake (Uranyl Acetate)
Body HTML:
The term “yellowcake” is probably familiar to most as something that’s used for making nuclear bombs. That’s really not too far from the truth (though in vastly more cases it’s earmarked for nuclear power production) but the term itself is informal and refers to more than one chemical.

Regardless of which specific substance we’re talking about, yellowcake always refers to a byproduct of uranium ore refining. As it comes out of the ground, uranium ore looks, and almost entirely actually is, just like any other ordinary looking rock but with a minor component of radioactive trace elements. To get to the good stuff, as a first step this rock is usually liquefied with acid. With uranium’s density being much higher than any of the other mineral components, this makes it easy to simply divert the topmost layers to the garbage heap out back and collect the heavy sludge at the bottom.

This heavy sludge is now of course much higher in uranium content but still has many impurities. To get rid of them, the refinery will usually add solutions containing ammonia which yields a yellowish residue from whence the term comes. This yellowcake is primarily ammonium diuranate with chemical formula (NH4)2U2O7 and this is usually the last step that the guys at the mine facilities will perform before packing it and sending it off to, hopefully, a properly accredited nuclear agency. They in turn will undertake the more arduous processing steps to get to the eventually tiny percentage of uranium-235, the fissionable isotope, that they need for running their reactors.

Clearly, there’s little expectation of seeing yellowcake for sale on Amazon. And, really, if you think about it, that’s a good thing considering it has very few legitimate uses for individuals. The only valid exception, in fact, would probably be for those shopping for unusual scientific collectibles. For them we’re glad to present this as an option for your stocking stuffer.

Remember kids, it’s safe only as long as it stays in the bottle! Treat it with the same respect as rat poison and everything’ll be alright.

Ok to ship internationally!

Title: Molybdenum Metal Cube 99.95%
Body HTML:
Molybdenum is an industrial metal that is usually used as an additive in steel. It is too rare and costly to play a significant role in pure form. As a powder it is toxic as it irritates skin and especially the lungs which could cause chemical pneumonia, a very serious condition. However, as a solid metal it is harmless and looks very similar to steel though considerably heavier.

This bright metal cube has satisfyingly sharp corners and is a true delight to hold. Keep it near your workspace and it'll put a smile on your face every time you reach for it!

Weight of each 10mm cube is 10 grams and for the 25.4mm approximately 163 grams.

Title: Tantalum Metal Cube 99.95%
Body HTML:
Tantalum is one of the most exotic metals that have everyday industrial applications. Your smartphone contains tiny capacitors that utilize it and though a negligible quantity per phone when multiplied by the hundreds of millions the demand becomes enormous. So valuable is this specialty metal that, sadly, its ores in Congo have become the focus of a ruinous war between rival gangs attempting to control its ownership. This war, the strong demand and the outright rarity of the metal make tantalum one of the most expensive industrial metals; gram per gram more expensive than silver!

10mm cubes weigh 16.7±0.3g.

25.4mm cubes weigh 274±1g.

Title: Tungsten Metal Cube 99.95%
Body HTML:
Tungsten takes the record for highest melting point of any metal and is also notable for being much heavier than lead (19 vs 13 grams per cubic centimeter). Stick it under the hottest torch you have laying around and this chunk of metal will giggle because it tickles. Torture it with acid and it'll yawn in boredom. Scratch it with another metal and it'll roll its eyes.

This truly beautiful cube was specially commissioned from a Chinese manufacturer that employs a hi-tech spark erosion process to give unbelievably tight tolerances and a stunning finish. If you've had any familiarity with tungsten chances are you'll know it to be a dark gray metal but with this machining process the metal is delightfully bright and reflective. And so hefty! Made from 99.95% pure tungsten each 10mm cube weighs 19.1 grams, the one inch cube 313±5g and the 50mm weighs a whopping 2300 grams (that's over five pounds!)

Title: Aluminum Metal Cube 99.9%
Body HTML:
To really appreciate how special aluminum is - a metal typically appreciated only by streetside recyclers - you should hold it next to one of the heavier cubes of the same size like tungsten. Compared this way they feel almost immaterial, like you could toss it in the air and it would float!
