Aluminum is the whitest of all the metals. Silver is more reflective but side by side silver appears, well, silvery where aluminum looks white, white, white. Of course, when highly polished those distinctions disappear - we're talking about appearance as normally encountered.

10mm cube weight is 2.7±0.1g and 25.4mm is 44g

Title: Copper Metal Cube 99.95%
Body HTML:
Copper, nature's only red metal! A kitchen full of polished copper pots and pans is a sight to behold and so are these cubes that you can't resist picking up and playing with. But, unlike the case with your pots, cleaning these delicate cubes is inadvisable. If you're clumsy enough to get ketchup on it use a cotton ball, water and a little prayer.

So yeah, it bears repeating: copper is delicate. Avoid touching it with your bare hands. The oils and acids will stain it permanently. Even leaving it out in open air will eventually.... have you seen the Statue of Liberty?

10mm cube weight is 8.9±0.5g.

25.4mm cube weight is 146±3g.

50mm cube weight is ~1,125g.

Title: Zinc Metal Cube 99.99%
Body HTML:
It's a pity zinc is such an unloved metal. It's the metal of shanty towns and garbage cans. But when a very pure block of zinc is milled to a polished sheen it has a downright classy look with the slightest tinge of blue.

It is a delicate luster so keep it lightly oiled or waxed as it otherwise tends to dull in air if left out for a long time.

10mm cube weight is 7.1±0.2g

Title: Lanthanum Metal Cube 99.5%
Body HTML:
Lanthanum is the black sheep of the rare earth family. It is both the most abundant component of the rare earth ores as well as relatively useless. Nearly all of the demand for it being taken up by toy sparklers and lighter flints. That's because when you grind it the little hot bits that fly off self-ignite in air in a fiery death before they reach the ground. A market for the metal was found in early versions of electric cars that had Ni-Cad batteries, where lanthanum played a role as minor constituent, but Elon Musk et al have snubbed that old tech for lighter lithium.

Lanthanum is also very susceptible to oxidation. Cut a piece in two to expose virgin metal and its luster will dull over the course of a few days until completely black. That thin coating of oxides is, unfortunately, insufficient to stop further degradation so the cubes must be kept stored under oil to stop the reaction from causing further damage. Except even in oil it will slowly continue to deteriorate unless the lanthanum and oil sit inside an airtight container. And by airtight we mean glass or metal as even plastic allows a limited passage of oxygen through.

As is the case with several other reactive metals on this page, we’ve handled the problem effectively by putting the cube inside an ampule with argon which is pretty much the only way you can hold on to this metal in a manner guaranteed to spare its delicate metallic luster while still being able to visually appreciate it.

10mm cube weight is 5.9±0.1g

Title: Gadolinium Metal Cube 99.9%
Body HTML:
Gadolinium is another one of the exotics known to chemistry majors as the lanthanides row and due to expense, difficulty in extraction and overall rarity it's remained little more than a lab curiosity. Our Chinese bulk suppliers have about as much affinity to this or any other rare earth metal as they do the steel drums they put them in. Perhaps if they saw them in these shiny perfectly straight-walled little cubes they'd regard them with a little more interest though, to be honest, the demographic slice that appreciates this sort of thing is, well... modest.

Weight of each 10mm cube is 8g.

Title: Neodymium Metal Cube 99.5%
Body HTML:
Now meet neodymium of strong magnet fame. Much like any other metal pure neodymium is gray as gray gets. That's pure neodymium. When it comes into contact with air - BAM! - oxygen pairs up with the cube's outermost atoms to form a black oxide. You can get rid of the oxidation with a stiff brush perhaps but, inevitably, it starts to darken and darken and darken some more until pitch black…. and then it starts to disintegrate into an inelegant pile of powder. Sad emoticon :-( 

But despair ye not! We’ve built this glass cage around the fragile neodymium cubes to ensure oxygen can do it no harm. You’ll be able to appreciate the metal’s metallic luster for as long as you don’t drop the thing onto the floor.

Title: Hafnium Metal Cube 99.95%
Body HTML:
If you were to divide the metals from the periodic table into two groups, those that are common and those that are rare, not only would hafnium be in the latter but if you were to divide them again you'd still find it on the rarer side of those two. Compound this natural rarity with low commercial demand and it's not difficult to see why hafnium is so expensive.

In nature hafnium is always found mixed up with zirconium and the two are nearly inseparable. However, their ratios are far from 50-50. In zirconium-rich sands the hafnium yield is on average only 2%. The two elements are so similar chemically that most commercial refineries don't even bother to extract out the hafnium. This means that unlike pretty much every other industrial metal the average zirconium part is a natural alloy containing between 1-3.5% hafnium!

Fortunately, zirconium was found to be a desirable material in the construction of fuel rods for the nuclear industry and that small percentage of hafnium was found to degrade its performance. Given this need several expensive purification processes have been employed to separate the two metals for this application. Today, nearly 100% of the hafnium metal being traded comes therefore thanks to the nuclear power industry's needing their zirconium ridded of the unwanted "contaminant".

These beautiful cubes are made from 99.95% pure pure hafnium. They feature perfectly sharp corners and flat, reflective walls. We are absolutely confident that you will be delighted with this collectible and that all who handle it will be wowed at its heft, its rarity and the precision of its craftsmanship.

Each 10mm cube weighs 13.5±0.1g and the 25.4mm weighs 221±2g

Title: Yttrium Metal Cube 99.95%
Body HTML:
Chemically, yttrium is a metal that belongs with the rare earth series despite its position on the periodic table next to the transition metals. This relatively obscure metal has few applications because it is difficult to refine, making it expensive, and machining it makes it exponentially more so because the heat generated by tooling instantly oxidizes the metal. Special processes are needed therefore to avoid this.

Yttrium is very close to neutral gray but has a hint of yellow to it. It’s fairly stable in air, turning only a little darker over the course of several days in open air which is only noticeable against a freshly cut piece. It does, however, oxidize much more rapidly if allowed to come into contact with water.

The weight of each 10mm cube is 4.5±0.1g.

Title: Carbon Cube 99.9%
Body HTML:
At first glance there doesn't seem to be anything remarkable about these cubes. But when you pick them up they feel so light that your brain does a bit of a double take - especially if you've been handling other metal cubes just before!

Ordinary graphite is quite cheap but while visually identical we specifically sourced the highest grade that was available, 99.99%, just because we all want it to be a little less ordinary, right? And there was not a significant bump in the cost which is always a nice bonus.

10mm cube weight is 1.7±0.1g.

25.4mm cube weight is 30.8±1g.

Title: Cobalt Metal Cube 99.95%
Body HTML:
Cobalt is an essential metal in heavy industry with many grades of steel containing significant quantities to improve wear and resistance to corrosion. And there's a whole laundry list of other areas where cobalt is used but, a little strangely, you never find pure cobalt objects. The fact that it costs ten times as much as copper might have something to do with it.

10mm cube weight is 8.9±0.05g

Title: Vanadium Metal Cube 99.95%
Body HTML:
Despite the dull gray of this metal in pure form, vanadium forms the most gorgeous set of colors when dissolved in acid. This was sufficient motive to earn it its name after the Norse goddess of beauty Vanadis. Less trivially, worldwide vanadium output is gobbled up almost entirely by the steel industry where adding a pinch of it to the mix provides wondrous increases in strength. Thus, vanadium-infused steel is preferentially used for structural work in any kind of large-scale construction. Indeed, among the strongest steels ever manufactured is an alloy known as W2 that comes with a tensile strength of a quarter million pounds per square inch! That is a mind-bogglingly strong metal and the record can only be accomplished through a generous injection of, you guessed it, vanadium.

This hi-tech steel is far too expensive to be used for such pedestrian things as rebar or railroad ties and is reserved instead for the priciest submarines or for critical parts in machinery. So why not use pure vanadium then? Well, as it turns out, that strength comes at a very serious ding in the machinability scale. It is a whopping 300 times more expensive than ordinary grades of steel, for one, but more importantly it is vastly more difficult to roll and shape into the desired end shapes. That added difficulty thus makes that W2 mentioned above a bargain in comparison.

Regardless, a cube made of 99.95% pure vanadium is a pretty cool addition to your collection of gadgets and we're sure you'll want to keep it handy!

The weight of each 10mm cube is 6.1±0.1g.

Title: Zirconium Metal Cube 97%
Body HTML:
Zirconium is a specialty metal with desirable properties but its use is limited due to cost and rarity. This high cost may come down with better methods of extraction as the earth has plenty of Zr but for now it remains just a minor player with a bright future.

The subtle yellow-brown tone of zirconium is difficult to appreciate on its own but quite evident when held up side by side to another metal with a more neutral gray. In our opinion it is one of the most attractively colored cubes that we offer.

10mm cube weight is 6.5±0.1g, 25.4mm weighs 106.5±1g

Title: Iron Metal Cube 99.9%
Body HTML:
Iron, the pure metal, is a deceptively difficult metal to find. The ordinary nail that you'd use on a two-by-four, and the head of the hammer you'd nail it with, are both made of "iron", yes, but both have small amounts of other elements added to improve various properties. A nail made of 99.9% pure iron such as offered in these cubes, for example, might be both more expensive (because of the added refinery steps required to get to this level or purity) and simultaneously more prone to breaking or bending all wrong when hammered. So practically nobody uses pure iron for anything. Yet, according to one industry report, a growing number of old school blacksmiths are requesting the pure stock for specialty projects like gates and artwork. Why? Because it turns out that purified iron resists rusting better than ordinary grades of steel. Now how's that for irony?

You wouldn't want to test this improved resistance to rust with this here expensive toy. Resistance does not equal foolproof. But on the other hand you can at least expect it to hold up better than, say, a bunch of nails from Home Depot!

Note that of all the nominally air stable metals iron is one of the most difficult to keep pristine because it is very prone to oxidation when pure

Weight for the 10mm cube is 7.7±0.1g

Title: Chromium Metal Cube 99.7%
Body HTML:
The ubiquity of chrome plating on plastic parts of every sort has infused an undeserved reputation for cheapness to this metal. This is because the chemical application of a thin coating of chromium is an effective way to prevent corrosion at the same time it provides a super-shiny surface.

Pure chromium metal on the other hand is an altogether different beast. It is exceptionally hard - perhaps the hardest of all pure metals - but it is also brittle making it unsuitable to make solid parts from. On the other hand it is so resistant to corrosion that injecting 10-20% into a blob of molten iron gives birth to "stainless steel". It's there literally under your nose every time you eat!

10mm cube weight is 7.4±0.1g and 25.4mm weight is 121±2g.

Title: Magnesium Metal Cube 99.95%
Body HTML:
The lightest structural metal is magnesium and although it's pretty soft the addition of a few trace elements makes it strong enough to turn into cameras, laptop frames and even into car engines for VW Beetles back in the 50's and 60's. Unfortunately, however tempting it may be to use magnesium as a replacement of steel, which is over three times as heavy, there are some considerable disadvantages. First, even when doped with those strengthening trace elements magnesium remains a metal too soft to withstand much pressure or high temperatures and, worse, it can catch a fire that burns white hot which becomes downright explosive if you try to put it out with water. Them's some scary properties.

While magnesium is fairly cheap even in 99.95% purity like this cube it is difficult to cut and polish increasing its final cost considerably. However, having one with which to weigh off against a cube of tungsten makes for a very interesting comparison!

Weight for the 10mm cube is 1.74±0.05g and 28.5±0.2g for the 25.4mm

Title: Cadmium Metal Cube 99.99%
Body HTML:
The media treats cadmium as a poisonous heavy metal, and not without good reason, as small amounts find their way into the water table due to industrial pollution, electronics waste and poor mining practices. This heavy metal then reacts chemically with the environment creating some quite toxic byproducts harmful to humans and wildlife alike.

But as long as you don't lick this cube like a lollipop there's no big warnings to heed and is safe to handle with your bare hands. The metal is bright gray with the merest hint of blue, is quite heavy but also soft thus resembling lead in both its physical characteristics as well as its toxicity profile.

10mm cube weight is 8.3±0.1g.

25.4mm cube weight is 136±2g.

Title: Tin Metal Cube 99.9%
Body HTML:
Phrases like 'tin foil' and 'tin cans' give the impression that tin is some cheap and disposable metal but in reality these are simply outdated holdovers from the 1930's and prior. The tin was quietly replaced with aluminum and steel because they were cheaper and more practical but consumers never got the memo so they kept using the antiquated terms.

Tin is actually not all that cheap and is pretty rare in commerce except for solder and pewter; both of which are low melting alloys. However, for all effects and purposes can only be obtained as a commodity from refineries and their distributors (or laboratories) because there's just no remaining use for the metal in pure form.

Each 10mm cube weighs 7.15±0.05g. The 25.4mm cube weighs 115 grams.

Title: Titanium Metal Cube 99.5%
Body HTML:
What's more valuable than gold and platinum? Titanium, if you go by credit card marketers who apparently figure this must be the ultimate mark of exclusivity. In truth, titanium is dirt common. Sand common to be more exact. Go to any beach and a handful of sand is bound to have some rutile which is titanium's main ore.

If there was any doubt about it being a common metal consider that hundreds of thousands of tons of titanium each year wind up in.... wait for it.... paint cans! Titanium oxide is what gives paint its opaque body. A perfect white for hospital halls and, of course, every wall in every house and building since all paints start out as white and are turned into all the colors of the rainbow only after dyes are swirled in.

Not to say that titanium is cheap per se. It is ten times costlier than steel, for the raw material, and its machining requires special equipment and training that drives up the cost of machined parts that much more.

But not so expensive that picking up a cube or two is going to be too upsetting to your wallet ;-)

10mm cube weight is 4.5±0.2g, 75g for the 25.4mm and ~570g for the 50mm.

Title: Antimony Metal Cube 99.95%
Body HTML:
Not possible, they said. Antimony doesn't behave like a metal, it can't be polished. I begged to differ, showing them a hand polished mirror I made from a sliced piece. No, no. That can't be done in a lapping machine. It will break the cubes and make a mess. 

Do it anyway, I said.

Well, I won the argument with our manufacturer and this is the result. And you know what? I think those battle scars are lovely. Now I want a whole mess of these little cubes so full of character. Maybe there's a mental health issue at play here but don't the little gouges and chips add a little character and set them apart from the rest of the gang?

Hooray for individuality!

10mm cube weight is 6.7±0.2g and 25.4mm is 110g.

Title: Bismuth Metal Cube 99.99%
Body HTML:
This is bismuth like you've never seen. Here at its boxiest, bismuth is a rather brittle element that despite looking at first glance like any other metal dropping a piece on the floor is likely to shatter it like glass. It melts easily but you wouldn't be able to get it cast into this shape as cavities tend to form on the wall of the mold and milling it would again cause surface fractures.

Bismuth is best known for the terraced crystals it forms like the one shown here on top of the 50mm version of the cube. The Escher-like shapes happen because the crystal solidifies from its molten state at different speeds. As one wall outpaces another it forces the creation of junctions at 90 degree angles. The colors are actually a result of oxidation. Although just a few microns thick the varying thicknesses each absorb and reflect different wavelengths of light thus creating a full palette of gorgeous colors.

The 10mm weighs 9.8g, the 25.4mm 160g and the 50mm clocks in at over 1,200 grams. Bismuth crystals sold separately.

Title: Scandium Metal Cube 99.9%
Body HTML:
The world at large has never heard of scandium. And that's a good thing because this obscure element has such desirable traits that were it to gain sudden fame the world's entire output would vanish overnight into the hands of a handful of wealthy commodity traders. Already as it is scandium is worth some fifty times as much as silver but - and here's the rub - all the scandium recovered annually is measured not in tons but in pounds. Buy a pound, if you can find it, and your little stash instantly becomes one of the world's largest stockpiles of this metal!

Light as aluminum but stronger than steel, this idyllic combination would be a dream building material for structural engineers. If only it wasn't so rare and expensive.

These cubes are made of 99.9% pure scandium metal and come with mirror polishing by default. A matte surface finish is also available upon request.

Weight of each 10mm cube is 3.0±0.03g. The 25.4mm cube is 49 grams.

Title: Nickel Metal Cube 99.95%
Body HTML:
This hard-wearing metal is a staple of the steel industry. Because of its hardness it makes for a great metal from which to make tools and coins and machine parts that encounter friction but its use as pure metal is less common because it is more expensive than steel while not significantly harder.

10mm cube weight is 8.7±0.1g.

25.4mm cube weight is 143±1g.

50mm cube weight is ~1,1kg

Title: Manganese Metal Cube 99.99%
Body HTML:
At first glance this ugly cube seems unworthy of a place in a collection. They may come with . But the battle-scarred little things have a story to tell.

Manganese is a rather ordinary and plentiful metal that gets little attention outside of the engineering offices of your average steel refinery. Large amounts are used in the making of many different types of alloys where it helps soak up excess contaminants, among other desirable traits.

But go ahead. Look for a piece of anything made out of pure manganese metal and you won't find it. Google manganese metal and you'll get page after page of industrial, butt-ugly looking things that look like something you'd scrape off your shoe while wincing. Attractive looking manganese metal bits and widgets are just about as common as rocking horse poop.

Why?

Manganese, it turns out, is an exceptionally difficult metal to obtain in high purity OR machine into a recognizable shape when in its pure state. The reason is locked up in arcane details of chemistry the upshot of which is that while the metal is perfectly docile at room temperature when heated it becomes highly susceptible to oxidation. And almost every type of machining operation involves friction, which of course creates heat... which readily ruins the Mn sample. 

The making of this high-purity cube therefore required overcoming two major challenges. First, it needed to be purified beyond the usually seen grades of 98-99%. As mentioned above, one of manganese's most desirable properties is that it scoops up contaminants from steel alloys. This means that divorcing it from its catch is going to take a lot of effort. Secondly, to overcome the problems created through friction, the lab has resorted to a fancy process called sintering. Usually used with rare precious metals, sintering involves chemically treating the material to render it into fine powder. It is then scooped up into an oversized mold which is hydraulically pressed on all sides while simultaneously subjected to high temperatures (and in the case of manganese in particular doing so in the absence of oxygen). The result is a block that appears every bit an ordinary, well, block of metal. And that’s it. Each one will receive its finishing touches to remove small imperfections and also the laser engraving to christen it with the ID from the periodic table but at that point it’s pretty much ready for primetime.

What? Wait, what’s then with those brown stains that make it look like rusting war relics and the rough finish that make it look more like masonry than a collectible? Well, consider the alternative. A prettier version - one with mirror polishing even - would need to be alloyed with some other metal otherwise the dreaded friction of polishing would ruin it.. In the end we’d rather anything but lose purity. The rusting blocks will grow on you. Builds character ;-)

One last thing. Sintering robs density because all that powder is pushed into a solid but still leaves millions of microscopic pores within. For this reason the mass of each 10mm cube is only about 6.2g (92±1g for the 25.4mm) which is about 10% lower than pure manganese would otherwise be.

Title: Sulfur Cube 99.9%
Body HTML:
From a distance these cubes made of pure sulfur look rather exactly like they're made of pure butter. Looks can be deceiving. When you hold them in your hand they feel somewhere between hard plastic and ceramic - and are, in fact, about as fragile as clay if not even more so.

Consider this one in the "better than nothing" category. We can't offer 100% perfect cubes like the others on this page. As you can see each cube has small imperfections like pockmarks and small chips on the edges and corners. These imperfections are because these cubes are cast, not machine cut. The molten sulfur conforms to the edges of the mold but traps many bubbles along the way which show up as tiny potholes. On the other hand, at least they don’t cost much and are a colorful addition in a collection of otherwise mostly gray members of your collection :-)

Title: Indium Metal Cube 99.99%
Body HTML:
It's hard to focus on writing up a description of indium without homing in on its one immediately obvious characteristic: its softness. While various web sources on elements claim for a number of metals that they're so soft that they "can be cut with a knife" indium is actually the only air-stable metal that actually can be cut with a knife. However, you'd still need a really sharp, big knife and strong muscle to do so if you wanted to slice through anything thicker than a finger-sized bar of it. Tin snips would be a better tool for this purpose.

This is the problem with this rare metal. It isn't, to be sure, quite as soft as play-doh - it's not like you can knead it with your fingers - but it's not that far off either. It's a neat trick to buy a rebar-sized rod and easily bend it as though you were the Hulk but it's a double-edged sword. If you were to pick up these cubes with anything harder than fleshy fingertips, like a tool for example, you'd instantly feel the metal giving way.

Which brings up another problem. Making these cubes is a labor of love, or a nightmare, depending on your point of view. It's trivially easy to get one of its six sides right - perfectly flat and polished even - but how do you support it so that you can work on the other sides without messing up the one you just got done? The answer is disappointingly low-tech: you simply have to be very very careful. Not to mention patient. The cubes are sliced through roughly and from that point forward treated as gingerly as possible. Our supplier spent many hours perfecting the technique to arrive at those desirable, perfect 90-degree corners but each one still takes a long time to make. The work that goes into making them, rather than the commodity price of the raw material, is what makes these so costly.

Ships in cotton-lined foam for protection. Please note that you will need to use extreme care in handling if you want to preserve their delicate shape. Even a minor bump into a hard surface will deform it at the point of impact. The edges and corners are so fragile that if you look at them wrong they will instantly smoosh in! 

10mm cube weighs 7.45g±0.05g and the 25.4mm tips the scales at 185g±2g

Title: Tellurium Metal Cube 99.99%
Body HTML:
The metalloid tellurium is a lot rarer in nature than the price suggests; comparable to gold but far cheaper due to lower demand. That disparity between rarity and cost however is slowly erasing as tellurium has found various uses in electronics.

This element is also quite brittle and its manufacture into the unyielding geometry of a cube has given our lab quite a few headaches. This, their fourth version, is far from perfect but is nearing the best that is possible without resorting to individually crafting each piece and making the whole thing inaccessible because of too high a cost. Note the rather crude level of polishing and imperfect edges. This isn't sloppiness on the part of the manufacturer but rather the result of flecks of material chipping away from the plane of grinding. Where normally grit passes over a surface and leaves behind a smooth surface in the case of Te it creates microscopic potholes because of its crystalline nature. Finally, tellurium is one of a handful of metals that is truly, absolutely gray.

The weight of each 10mm cube is 6.12±0.03g and just over 100 grams for the 25.4mm one.

Title: Lutetium Metal Cube 99.95%
Body HTML:
On the lanthanides row of the periodic table element Lu sits at the far end both in a literal and symbolic sense. It is easily the rarest and most expensive rare earth metal (unless we count scandium). Its richest commercial ore, monazite, has a yield of just 0.0001%. Given its density of nearly 10 grams per cubic centimeter this means that it took 100 kilograms of ore to make just one of these little cubes!

Rarity and expense combine to leave lutetium as one of the most obscure and least commercially useful of all the stable metals. It does, however, make for one heck of a conversation piece!

Weight for the 10mm cube is 10.1±0.05g.

Title: Germanium Cube 99.999%
Body HTML:
Germanium is another late but welcome member to our lineup of density cubes. Our initial order for these was made back in December 2016 but the manufacturer found it very challenging to make the cubes and we had to go through several prototypes before we struck gold (so to speak). And, actually, we have to accept some minor chipping along the edges and corners. The combination of machine grinding and a brittle material equals high failure rates. Despite all of these problems, or perhaps because of them, we are happy indeed to finally have them in inventory as we were doubting we'd ever have a presentable Ge density cube.

Yes, they really are slightly greenish as in the photo! This is this element's color :-)

Each 10mm cube weighs 5.3±0.01g and the 25.4mm ones are 87±0.2g

Title: Erbium Metal Cube 99.95%
Body HTML:
Here we have another metal that is virtually unknown to all who walk this planet. Erbium is another member of the Rare Earths metal group. Occupying the top of the bottom two rows on the periodic table, they aren't all that rare in nature but they sure are so when it comes to finding them at your local hardware store. And yet this isn't entirely accurate. Most electronic devices have small amounts of various REs performing specialized functions because other elements can't do the job as well.... but talk about flying under the radar!

While the whole group behaves similarly at the molecular level, the REs have different characteristics. Some oxidize in air rapidly, some are soft while others are hard, you have heavy rare earth metals and there are light ones and so on.

Erbium flies under the radar even for the rare earth group. It has no record-breaking properties among its cousins. It is not especially reactive (can be kept indefinitely in open air without tarnishing), it is relatively heavy at 9g per cubic centimeter (but two others are heavier) and is slightly rarer than the average RE. Just about the only distinctive thing about Er is that its salts are colored a girly pinkish hue. Find expensive pink glassware and chances are that you're looking through a few billion atoms of our friend erbium :-)

Weight for the 10mm cube is 9.4±0.05g.

Title: Samarium Metal Cube 99.99%
Body HTML:
These Sammies kick off our Rare Earth metal density cube series. It wasn't lack of interest in the Lanthanides that's kept them til so late in the game but rather expense. While our suppliers have been eager to please with small runs and even some free prototypes of the more common metals the REs were a different story. Many don't want to handle them at all, citing lack of technical know-how while others demanded steep upfront investment and few wanting to commit to any sort of quality control. With those kinds of caveats it's hard to jump in willingly.

But jump in we did - and to the deep end of the pool! We've ironed out the wrinkles and are ready to offer high-quality density cubes that are every bit as superbly finished as the others in our lineup.

As a raw commodity, samarium is one of the more abundant metals that comes out of the cocktail of rare earths ores euxenite, monazite and other exotic -ites that yield the little-known metals group. However, unlike most of the rest of this 14-strong gang, samarium has strong demand in making industrial grade magnets. This skews the price and gives the impression that it's rarer than it actually is in nature.

Like lead or copper, samarium is shiny when first made but soon turns dark gray. Unlike those two, however, it retains some of its luster indefinitely. The laser engraving is also unique in that it appears light yellow… an unintentional byproduct of oxides forming under the brief heat of the laser!

10mm cube weight is 7.5±0.05g.

Title: Terbium Metal Cube 99.9%
Body HTML:
Terbium is one of the rarer "rare earth" metals; a minor component of major ores monazite and euxenite which are actively mined, especially in China, in search of just these exotic elements. Despite its poor familiarity with the public there is a strong demand for terbium in the making of LCD panels as one of its salts lights up green. Turn your screen to a full image of a jungle and the light reaching your eye has been filtered through countless atoms of this metal.

It's now time to roll out the red carpet for this metal by introducing these little well-made cubes. Highly purified terbium (99.9% to be exact) has been melted for us into a large sheet then rough sawn to oversize cubes. The supplier then has them sculpted with a high speed stylus while immersed in an electrolytic bath. It is this step that brings the very high degree of parallelism that results in corners meeting at an exact 90 degrees with laser sharp edges. The semi-finished cubes then go for a gingerly polishing step that sees them each spin in a lapping machine for hours to bring out their shine. Finally, they are engraved with a laser with their atomic number, symbol and number. They are then ready to be flown to the USA where we individually package them to get them ready for our customers. Ta da!

10mm cube weight is 8.4±0.2g.

Title: Calcium Metal Cube 99.9%
Body HTML:
Calcium is a very unusual metal to be formed into density cubes – or anything at all for that matter. In fact, to our knowledge these cubes are the first time pure calcium has been made into a commercial product. 

There are several good reasons for this. Calcium is not stable in air and will oxidize into a powdery mess if left out. In a previous writeup we had penned that the metal is too soft to be useful. That's not entirely correct. It's not particularly soft, it just has poor structural integrity. Apply physical stress to a piece of calcium and it quickly crumbles. That's not softness per se, it's just poor cohesion. In any case, that's definitely not a trait you look for in a metal.

Calcium is much too reactive to be found in nature in its elemental state. To keep it pure it must be stored in mineral oil or, preferably, an inert gas. We are now offering them encased in a glass ampule under argon where it is guaranteed to not degrade as long as the glass doesn’t break.

Title: Ytterbium Metal Cube 99.9%
Body HTML:
Ytterbium is another metal destined to remain obscurity for the foreseeable future, silently filling minor industrial roles along with all the other elements in the lanthanide series of rare earth metals.

These Yibbies, as we've affectionately nicknamed the element at our office here, are made from triple-nine pure ytterbium metal and mark, so far as we know, the first time a density cube has been made of this material and offered for sale.

Weight of each 10mm cube is 6.7±0.5g.

Title: Dysprosium Metal Cube 99.5%
Body HTML:
Dysprosium: another exotic metal few have heard about. It is magnetic, neutral gray and reacts slowly with water tarnishing it into an oxide while freeing water's hydrogen atom. Despite what Wikipedia says about this element, it is most definitely not so soft that it "can be cut with a knife." Regarding rarity it comes towards the rarer end of the scale among the rare earth metals group with which it is associated and, not surprisingly, it is fairly expensive in pure form.

A cube made out pure dysprosium metal has probably never, not once figured on any kid's wish list for Santa but now that Luciteria is making them who knows?

10mm cube weight is 8.6±0.05g

Title: Holmium Metal Cube 99.9%
Body HTML:
Trivia fans will stump all but the most die-hard of geeks with the question "What element on the periodic table named after a country's capital?" Never mind that a few years ago a couple of atoms of moscovium were made in a lab, the honor to such a question has for the longest time gone to holmium which would have been less trivial if it had been named stockholmium but we're getting sidetracked....

This rare earth metal is remarkably free of properties that could be exploited by industrialists but it finds its way into myriad small uses anyway. Metals do not all have to have ideal characteristics in order to be useful after all and holmium, like the rest of the lanthanides, is extremely difficult to isolate so that once it has been refined there are still markets willing to put the little-known metal to suitable tasks.

10mm cube weight is 8.9±0.1g.

Title: Silver Metal Cube 99.9%
Body HTML:
Our insistence that the manufacturer use lab-grade silver of 99.9% purity rather than the cheaper but more workable silver alloy used in jewelry making delayed the delivery of our first batch of cubes by several months.

Silver is especially susceptible to tarnishing via airborne, microscopic particles containing sulfur. Like fine silverware, it is more or less inevitable that it will tarnish in open air sooner or later depending primarily on its proximity to sulfur which is a common air pollutant. To keep them shiny it’s recommended to store them in an airtight container. However, should they turn dark a brief wash in jewelry cleaner fluid should restore the shine.

Weight of the 10mm cubes are 10.5±0.02g for the 10mm’s and 174±1g for the 25.4mm.

Title: Beryllium Mirror Cube 99.5%
Body HTML:
Sometimes the story behind the subject is more interesting than the subject itself. These beryllium cubes are a good example.

In early 2017 we began commissioning a seemingly simple project. Turn the grid of a periodic table into a real-life showcase with each cell occupied not by text, or even a picture of what that text represented, but by actual samples of the elements. And to remain true to the format let's make them cubes so they can be arranged in the same manner, shoulder to shoulder, that one can easily compare the visual and tactile differences between them.

Of that theoretical 63 the greater part pose little challenge to experienced metalsmiths. We found that the same manufacturers who make brass door hinges can just as easily use pure copper to make cubes, the most basic of three dimensional shapes. An auto parts manufacturer has access to nickel, iron, titanium and so on as well as the equipment to make cubes of those materials. Not surprisingly those easy metals proved to be the first cubes we carried a few months ago. Then things got progressively more... interesting.

To make a long story short, beryllium has come very late in our lineup for a number of reasons. We contacted some 30 manufacturers who had experience with this rare metal only to be disappointed time and again. Where there was interest there was typically the expectation that we would place an order on the scale of a Walmart. In other cases an initial deposit was cheerfully accepted only for weeks to drag on to months until finally only a sheepish apology and refund was provided. One of our last promising leads gave up after two months telling us, dryly, that an expensive piece tool had been completely ruined in the effort. Wooops.

A sole manufacturer was dogged enough to keep trying. Luckily, they were as persistent as we were. Their initial efforts were crude, with a poor level of polish and loose tolerances. But they kept at it thanks to a technician who enjoyed the challenge. And, finally, nearly three months after our company began discussions with them they delivered these absolutely stunning GEMS, for there really is no other appropriate adjective with which to describe them!

Beryllium, getting back to the subject as opposed to the story behind the subject, is an exotic and expensive metal that is very difficult to refine. It is also technically challenging to machine it into shapes because it tends to wreck normal steel tools and the powdered bits of metal that result from such machining is poisonous.

Fortunately, our supplier has perfected the technique. Unfortunately, as you can see, the result is far from affordable. However, for your hard-earned money you can at least be sure that you are getting an absolutely enviable collectible in a flawless “package”.

Each perfectly made beryllium density 1cm cube weighs 1.80±0.03g. The inch cube weighs an ounce. Satisfaction absolutely guaranteed!

Title: Selenium Metal Cube 99.99%
Body HTML:
This is not an element that easily submits to strict geometry. It is most similar to porcelain: brittle and ceramic-like. It melts easily and forms puddles, like tar or licorice candy, and as it cools down it turns to a taffy-like material. Upon further cooling it hardens and thin strands give way and break like cheap plastic. But what it definitely does not like to do is take the form of a cube. Our first version, sold from 2015 through the end of 2020, was cast into molds and left to harden in-situ but selenium's "personality" protested and within the confines of its squarish cells only loosely approximated the desired final form. Although in the end the result was attractive in its own way we were unsatisfied with not achieving a true cube that could furthermore accept the laser engraving we felt necessary to complete inclusion into the family of density cubes we offer.

The technical hurdles that were overcome were not in truth very difficult, just laborious. The new technique still uses casting as a first step but a much bigger piece forms a so-called bolus (sounds better than "formless blob") which provides a starting point. From here a special saw whose diamond-tipped blades turn at a very slow speed is allowed to cut slives at exactly 10mm apart. And you can see where this is going. The critical step is the low-speed sawing which prevents this brittle material from the chipping which would inevitably occur with an ordinary saw or similar cutting tool. Once all the slicin' and dicin' is done the laser engraving step is a piece of cake ;-)

10mm weight is 4.1±0.2g.

Title: Thulium Metal Cube 99.9%
Body HTML:
The inorganic chemistry textbooks treat the rare earth metals as if they were all clones of each other. Chemically they're all very alike and this is the main reason why they're so hard to isolate from the ores in which they're all lumped together. The literature, however, could do a better job of pointing out that this doesn't make them interchangeable. Their physical characteristics, for one, are quite differentiated. 

Thulium is one of the rarer of this class of metals and while nothing about it looks-wise is remarkable it turns out that it is especially difficult to prepare into shaped forms. Our supplier, whose specialty is rare earth metals, had no idea that this was the case. No customer had ever asked for something similar before. They were mystified that this metal resisted machining despite being ductile and reasonably resistant to oxidation. Specifically, the various techniques used with the other rare earth metals were not working out in this case. It took weeks of trial and error before the engineers found a solution that could be applied at an industrial scale. And even then, if you note carefully, the surface of the cubes shows speckling and brush marks... vestiges of ill-behaved metal that does not wish to conform into a perfectly cubic shape ;-)

Each 10mm thulium cube weighs 9.3±0.1g.

Title: Gold Mirror Cube 99.99%
Body HTML:
Like fire that has been frozen solid, gold has no equal in history to mesmerize and becraze men the world over. Rare, beautiful and easy to work with, this metal will always be in high demand. It will be, in fact, synonymous with money and desirability.

And speaking of desirability, these little 10mm cubes will without any doubt command the most attention of anyone looking at the other fifty-something cubes we offer. Each cube is perfectly proportioned and lovingly finished to be equal parts precision engineered scientific reference and jewelry. 

Each 10mm cube weighs approximately 19.25 grams. Larger cubes can be made as a custom order at a 21.5% markup to the current spot price.

Title: Osmium Metal Cube 99.95%
Body HTML:
How to describe osmium. That's an interesting question. For a metal this classy using cheesy superlatives seems, well, cheesy. And yet the metal is so deserving of grandiose adjectives because rarest, most valuable, heaviest and hardest are four such terms that truly do apply in rendering a description.

So let's start by describing osmium as record breaker on the periodic table:

- At 22.59 grams per cubic centimeter it is the densest of all naturally occurring elements

- It vies with its cousins rhenium and iridium as the rarest naturally occurring, stable element with an estimated concentration of only 1 part per billion in earth's crust.

- For any given designed shape it is the most expensive metal on the market

- It is the least compressible of all types of solid matter excepting neutron stars, black holes and similar. It cannot be crushed.

- In the Olympics of the periodic table osmium gets the bronze medal behind diamond (carbon) and chromium in terms of hardness; and a silver for metal with the second highest melting point (behind tungsten).

Clearly, Mother Nature was proud of her 76th invention and decided to hoard most of it while leaving barely a trace for humanity. In fact, most people have never heard of osmium. And that's a good thing because it concerns one more record breaker: the market. All the osmium produced in the world yearly would fit in the palm of your hand.

Let that sink in.

If everyone wanted a little osmium what would happen to the price? It's already as expensive as gold but is at least ten thousand times rarer commercially. If I were a billionaire and wanted to buy a ton of gold I could literally buy it online and have it delivered the next day. If I called a broker in precious metals to please locate for me one ton of osmium at any price he'd get back to me sheepishly apologizing that, sir, there is just not enough quantity available.

And this brings us finally to these two little guys on the left. Pure osmium metal is available to the public in many forms. There is the powder (potentially toxic), arc-melted beads, crystals and the granules we sell right here on Luciteria. But, unless Google's failing me, never has anyone actually designed a formed object in this element until we came along commissioning the first cubic centimeter piece. 

It was a tall order for the suppliers in our network because they had no working experience with osmium and the design specs called for absolute perfection. 10 millimeters to a side with a deviation of no more than a tenth of a millimeter and all six sides polished to a satiny sheen. Most importantly, none of the edges or corners could be chipped. We were pleasantly surprised with one initial quote for $1,200 per piece only to have the sales lead cancel the order unceremoniously a few days later and reissue the quote at a much less attractive offer. In all, we asked over twenty labs and other specialty firms across the world and most came back with bad news of one sort or another.

The wait was worth it. Our first two cubes pictured here are nothing short of an engineering marvel. The team that made it deserves a standing ovation. In fact, an obscure precision metals workshop in Beijing can take quiet pride in knowing that they gave not just their nation but the world as a whole the very first manufactured items made out of pure osmium metal. Well done!

Osmium cube of 10mm weighing 20.8±0.3 grams and 25.4mm ranging between 335-350g. Larger sizes available if you or a family member is mentioned in Forbes magazine.

Title: Violet Phosphorus Cube 99.99%
Body HTML:
Our 61st addition to our element cube product line is phosphorus. Although a very useful material phosphorus has come afoul of US regulations because it has unfortunately been found to be a key ingredient in making meth. So thanks to low rent junkies the procurement of this otherwise basic element is artificially difficult and expensive to acquire.

Typically seen as the dark red strip on matchbooks, phosphorus comes as a red powder that looks like pulverized terracotta and smells like garlic bread. Unlike the clay that it looks like it decidedly doesn't want to be formed into shapes. Merely clumping it together works much the same way as does dry sand. Our first cubes used a binding agent which did the trick well but at the expense of both density and overall purity. Starting in late 2021 a new method solved this tricky problem and finally achieved essentially pure phosphorus. While the lab did not disclose the process the likely solution was to subject the starting powder to extreme pressure and heat under in an airless environment to prevent them from auto-igniting.

Your kitchen matches and these cubes are basically one and the same thing. Treating them with the same care as any other highly flammable material ensures your set doesn’t turn into an unwelcome 4th of July show. Just keeping them in the included acrylic box for long term storage should be enough but as an added dash of caution keep them well away from sources of heat too.

These cubes are hygroscopic; that is, they attract water. When kept inside their storage in an area of high humidity you may notice some “dew” forming inside. Just taking the lid off for a minute or two will dry them out again.

Title: Thallium Metal Cube 99.99%
Body HTML:
This right here is the absolute ultimate exotic addition to our cubes lineup. Thallium is the only downright scary element from the periodic table this side of radioactive materials you only hear about following nuclear catastrophes. It is estimated that as little as 1.3 grams of this metal ingested is enough to put you six feet under. If it were only lethal when swallowed that would be bad enough but thallium has the (extremely unfortunate for human life) ability to pass through unbroken skin. That makes simply holding it hazardous to one’s health and the main reason we don’t stock the metal in its raw form.

As if the chemical hazards were not enough to contend with, thallium poses a slew of technical challenges to bedevil an engineer contemplating fashioning gadgets out of it. It is both very soft and vulnerable to oxidation. Working with it means either consigning expensive equipment for sole thallium use or laborious processes to prepare and then afterwards decontaminate the tools used. All of this, and not least of which is the expense of the metal itself, contributed to this being one of the last elements that we’ve turned into a perfect cubic centimeter.

While we learned a lot during the manufacturing process in the end only a handful of these cubes were completed over the course of eight long months, a phonebook’s worth of emails and the coordination of two labs in different continents! We're proud of the end results which leaves us with gleaming, six-sided mirrors of pure thallium - an object that will impart awe and respect in equal measures.

It really should go without saying but of all our items this deserves the sternest precaution be taken for anyone considering buying one of these. This is not the sort of disclaimer to be taken lightly. Kept in this reasonably sturdy glass ampule you are safely shielded from one of nature’s deadliest concoctions by a mere millimeter’s worth of glass. It is a transcendental feeling to be this close to something so lethal made harmless by such a trivial divider. Its owner should treat it with exactly the same level of respect you’d have for, say, slicing through fugu fish or being handed a test tube full of Ebola. We’re not kidding.

Title: Wood and Glass Display Case for 10mm Sets
Body HTML:
Please note this display case does not fit the 14mm acrylic case. It is meant for the now discontinued 10mm trays pictured.

This custom display case enhances and protects the acrylic tray that comes with the acrylic cell tray for the 10mm metal cubes. The hinged glass lid comes with a soft magnetic seal and is lined in red felt. Measures 10.5” x 6” x 1.5”.

Note that this is for the wooden case only. The acrylic tray and density cubes are sold separately.

Title: Aluminum Mirror Polished Cube
Body HTML:
Aluminum is a metal that polishes to a highly reflective surface very easily and, considering how cheap it is, should make this one of the most affordable offerings on this page. But it isn’t. The reason is that aluminum is also quite soft and requires great care when grinding and polishing to prevent damage. Unfortunately, it is only very late in the process, after the lapping machine has completed its cycle, that the individual cubes can be examined for damage. The smallest of variables, if not simply bad luck, is enough to bump the cube enough that a tiny error - one that would go unnoticed in the regular seven dollar cubes - sticks out to mar an otherwise perfect specimen.

We’re happy to offer them in 10mm as well as 1- and 2-inch sizes.

Title: Bismuth Mirror Cube
Body HTML: Bismuth is a tricky metal to polish to the point you can see your face in it. Although it is pretty soft, which aids in the process, it is also quite brittle. Where lapping machines can be dialed in to “set and forget” with bismuth one must go through additional steps of progressively finer grits to avoid the abrasives from simply ripping out material and spoiling the surface being ground. It is largely the extra labor, again, that accounts for the relatively high cost of these cubes.

Title: Cadmium Mirror Cube
Body HTML:
If tin and lead had a baby they’d have named it cadmium. It has the former’s heft and the latter’s softness. Like lead it is also dangerous to health if ingested and becomes a serious waterway pollutant if allowed to degrade in the environment.

But a big solid piece of cadmium is safe to handle (not that a wash with soap and water afterwards would be a bad idea). It takes on a beautiful chrome-like polish but, again, is somewhat fragile given its softness. For this reason you’ll want to protect your investment by keeping it away from those afflicted with butter fingers!

Title: Carbon Satin Cube
Body HTML:
This, possibly the cheapest of all elements, turns out to be among the most beautiful when highly polished. Industrially supplied carbon (purified coal basically) is a relatively porous and low density material that is used in the production of high grades of steel. Even at the highest purity of 99.99% the stuff is only a couple bucks per kilogram. Optionally, after applying a lot of different steps to compact, shape, cut and polish it you get to the pricier yet still pretty cheap cubes we offer.

Applying further polishing, unfortunately, does not yield a mirror cube. It simply leaves the dull dark gray cubes feeling smoother to the touch. In our quest to flesh out a mirror variant of each of our normally polished cubes we eventually overcame this problem by reformulating the starting material into a denser version with fewer pores and switching to a manual polishing stage rather than let the machine do it. Although the extra steps and manual labor ended up adding to the base price the jump was suprisingly modest. The premium look alone is worth the extra cost!

Title: Chromium Mirror Cube
Body HTML:
And now chromium. Perhaps the most deserving cube to get the upgraded polishing treatment as "chrome" is basically synonymous with reflective metal.

These guys were made using the same process as the more matte-looking surfaced chromium cubes but after going through the customary grind polishing stage they were sent to a flat lapping machine to bring out the extra shine. And with each the result is a little gem that will bring a smile whenever you hold it!

Title: Cobalt Mirror Cube
Body HTML: These beauties are a pleasure to hold in hand. Shame the finger prints though as you’ll be viciously buffing them out after handling. Perfect in every way, these cubes of pure cobalt metal are true gems of engineering!

Title: Copper Mirror Cube
Body HTML:
A six-sided mirror made out of solid copper has been a dream long in coming. It is an exceptionally difficult project primarily because copper tarnishes easily on top of being fairly soft. It is frustratingly easy during manufacture to ruin an edge and the smallest mistake during polishing results in unsightly stains.

We acquired a highly refined copper and laboriously buffed it to a satiny sheen. We then sawed it into sections using a special low speed saw to preserve the critical edges and finally buffed the two ends to match the satin finish. The cubes that had made it to this stage were then given the final polishing that achieved the fully reflective mirrors.

If you're considering buying one of these cubes it is highly recommended that you do not touch them with your bare fingers unless you first wash your hands with an industrial soap that gets every last trace of oil. Even still it's preferable to handle them with gloves and stored in an airtight container to avoid discoloration from oxides. If it gets dirty it can be wiped down with a cotton pad and some acetone or rubbing alcohol.

Title: Dysprosium Mirror Cube
Body HTML:
Along with neodymium, there has been steady growth in the demand for dysprosium as an important component of specialty magnets. These find their way into all sorts of consumer goods from smartphones to electric cars. With production sources pretty much all spoken for and sourced among just a few refineries in China there’s little expectation that the price will go anywhere but up - and it’s already pricey as is.

Making it even costlier, of course, is the mirror polishing treatment which takes several days of continuous grinding in a lapping machine. The result, besides the sticker shock that is, is a six-sided mirror made from this exotic metal. And they are absolutely beautiful!

Title: Erbium Mirror Cube
Body HTML:
Drab and colorless as iron, erbium shares little else with its ferrous cousin. It is expensive and difficult to isolate from its ores and, unlike most other metals, nearly all of it is mined and process only by a single country: China. Its ability to provide a unique tint of a very narrow band in the visible spectrum when added to glass has found demand for this metal in the making of exotic lasers and optical fibers. This latter being a key component of the explosion in demand for data pipelines has put a good strain on the availability of this metal. It certainly should make Chinese monopolies happy (and the buyers equally nervous).

These cubes were painstakingly polished over the course of many days to yield impressively reflective surfaces and will make for a peerless addition to anyone’s element collection.

Title: Gadolinium Mirror Cube
Body HTML:
This obscure rare earth metal polishes well AND keeps its luster in open air so long as you don’t get it wet (in which case it will leave a hazy stain). Placed next to a gray color reference, gadolinium appears to have a very slight yellow color.

Gadolinium is also the unlikely focus of a controversy within the medical world. It turns out that it’s especially effective in MRI scans. As the acronym hints at, MRIs do their magic by exploiting small differences in magnetic fields within the body. These differences can be mapped and then formed into an image which can then be used in diagnosing many kinds of diseases.

Gadolinium’s role in these machines functions as a contrast enhancer to make those images clearer. While the iron in blood is magnetic it’s literally everywhere in the body so it’s no use for this task. What’s needed is a material that is magnetic but only just enough of it, like a fine dusting, to reveal hidden outlines. Gadolinium, which is slightly magnetic, is an exotic material that has no natural reason to accumulate in tissues. When the patient drinks a mix containing a small quantity of gadolinium these particles disperse via the bloodstream and their unique signature can be picked up by the scanner delineating more clearly the boundaries of organs and whatever other tissues are being examined.

The controversy stems from what-if questions arising out of potential litigation. Being a substance normally alien to the human body - and even though apparently harmless in the small quantities used - trace amounts of Gd may continue lingering in the body long after the MRI scan served its purpose…. and potentially be used in litigation should the patient sprout a condition whose origins can’t be explained otherwise. Fear of malpractice lawsuits is therefore putting pressure on engineering workarounds to phase out the use of this metal.

But don’t feel too bad for the underdog. The demand for gadolinium in the medical industry is truly a drop in the bucket compared to its much larger market in the electronics and auto industries. Just don’t forget to take your gadolinium cube out of your pockets when you show up for your next MRI ;-)

Title: Hafnium Mirror Cube
Body HTML:
This unfortunate metal is evidently doomed to perennial obscurity. Extremely dense, costly, rare and supremely difficult to purify are all nails in this coffin. Hafnium is used, in small amounts, to make specialty alloys as well as in the nuclear industry but demand is relatively low as managers in these projects will always try to find alternatives to keep costs down.

Now, turning to fans of the periodic table, hafnium’s obscurity is precisely what makes it attractive to collectors. Our normally polished hafnium cubes are (not surprisingly) fairly pricey items in their own right. Adding mirror polishing boosts the price up even farther as it takes several days (yes, days) of continuous polishing to achieve a reflective surface. The result though is a perfectly made little jewel that brings immediate tactile and visual satisfaction to its owner!

Title: Holmium Mirror Cube
Body HTML: This little-known metal gets the royal treatment. As far as anyone can tell these two inch-sized cubes are the very first objects made of pure holmium metal that have been polished to be as reflective as a mirror. Perhaps some anonymous tech at one point took the effort to do it for some specialized project but if so there’s no photographic evidence of it. So we’re comfortable saying it’s the first time it’s been available commercially. Not cheap, true, but special things tend to be pricey!

Title: Iron Mirror Cube
Body HTML:
As mentioned previously, iron is not a metal one often comes across in ultra-pure grades. While it is the cheapest of the structural metals it oxidizes too easily and is fairly brittle. The addition of various other metals makes it much better suited for the tasks of being turned into bridges and cars but at that point it's no longer pure iron but rather steel.

Our supplier has provided us with prototypes of machine-made cubes that have additionally been polished to the degree you'd expect of chrome plating. Being however pure iron does mean that the elements can easily take their toll on this expensive keepsake. Iron's worst enemy is humidity, especially if it's the sort that comes off nearby seawater as it quickly creates a chemical reaction that results in rust. For this reason you will want to make sure that wherever you put this beautiful collectible it's somewhere dry!

Title: Lutetium Mirror Cube
Body HTML:
Lutetium is the heavy slugger of the lanthanide series. Hard, rare and about as dense as lead, this obscure metal has very few commercial uses primarily due to the high cost of production.

We’ve taken our pricey Lu centimeter cubes and given them the royal treatment to bring out the shine like nobody’s business. And now it’s even costlier, true, but in the extremely unlikely event that someone within earshot were to brag about their piece of pure lutetium you would win the contest in a most dramatic way :-D

Title: Molybdenum Mirror Cube
Body HTML: Molybdenum is an industrial metal which pretty much never gets any special attention. But this all changes with these lavishly polished cubes of perfect centimeter dimensions. They cost about five times as much as the regular cubes but are, arguably, ten times prettier!

Title: Magnesium Mirror Cube
Body HTML: Magnesium’s softness makes it easy to polish to a very high degree but this softness is also its Achille’s heel. If the lapping machine is not precisely set or the technicians decide to take a brief peek at their social media feeds - poof - the cube is ruined. The trouble doesn’t end there, unfortunately. Unlike its better known alloy type, pure magnesium also happens to be a very delicate metal with simple moisture being its primary enemy. If the cubes are left in a humid environment the water vapor dulls those beautifully polished surfaces with an irreversible, milky type of tarnish. To prevent this from happening avoid touching them with sweaty fingers and leave them in their protective cubes for long term storage.

Title: Nickel Mirror Cube
Body HTML: While superficially "gray" nickel is actually a metal that has a very elegant light brown tone that becomes obvious when placed side by side a grayer metal like iron. Because it is hard it is easily polished. Easy, however, in terms of the mechanics of polishing. All you need to do is polish it with progressively finer grit. A process that takes hours of tedious, manual work. But after all that toil you are rewarded with a blindingly reflective jewel that will inevitable draw the attention of anyone who happens to look at it. That we can absolutely guarantee!

Title: Niobium Mirror Cube
Body HTML:
Niobium sports all the colors of the rainbow if an oxide layer forms. But for this to happen it has to be almost incomprehensibly thin: on the order of a few nanometers. A nanometer or two is all it takes to change the color but too deep and the whole thing just turns an ugly black. Luckily, the process is easily controlled using electricity and an industry has popped up around niobium to give the world colorful metal jewelry (along with titanium which has similar properties).

We can also make niobium beautiful by simply polishing the bejeezus out of it until it resembles chrome plating!

Title: Samarium Mirror Cube
Body HTML: To prevent this sensitive rare earth metal from turning opaque each centimeter cube is placed inside a small glass ampule containing pure argon gas. When highly polished, samarium attains a gorgeous sepia tone similar to nickel.

Title: Scandium Mirror Cube
Body HTML:
Based on cost alone scandium could compete as a precious metal, easily besting silver and at times rivaling ruthenium and even platinum. While not terribly rare, scandium’s mining problem is that it’s never concentrated in deposits and ore veins the way other metals are and is, instead, recovered as mere impurities from the refining of other ores. The amount is truly puny. The monthly worldwide production of scandium has never been higher than a single ton or so - over a hundred times less than gold!

Scandium is a beautiful brassy hue. Combining the three key properties of high strength, lightness and resistance to oxidation, scandium would make an ideal building material if it wasn’t so expensive. Our first batch of these highly polished cubes broke another record in that they immediately sold out - to our own staff! That had never happened before and don’t expect it will again. Still in limited quantities, here’s your chance to score a true metal jewel!

Title: Silver Mirror Cube
Body HTML: If you can afford to splurge on only one mirror polished cube these silver ones would be an ideal choice. Costing much more than the regular ones, and waaaay more than their modest weight would be as bullion, the expense is justified in the extraordinary level of care and difficulty in getting a six-sided mirror to laser sharp edges. Each one is a masterpiece of engineering and we guarantee your total delight!

Title: Tantalum Mirror Cube
Body HTML:
The word tantalum is creeping into the news with greater frequency due to the metal’s importance as a key ingredient in electronics…. and the fact that the majority of it is mined in areas of Africa with little government oversight. This unfortunately associates it with the same problems as diamonds and other “conflict resources” the profits of which often end up in the pockets of warlords.

These gorgeously made cubes come from China. At over 16 grams each you’ll appreciate the heft of one of the universe’s toughest and densest metal. Lovingly polished to a mirror on each of its six sides you’ll also appreciate its classy, dark reflections that play magic with any light that shines upon it.

Title: Terbium Mirror Cube
Body HTML:
Few people outside of specialty occupations in chemistry and the electronics industry are familiar with terbium. Nevertheless, in recent years demand for the metal has grown steadily. Coupled with the high cost of preparing the metal, and its natural rarity, have pushed the price per kilogram well into the four digits.

Just as metals typically expand and contract with temperature variations, terbium has the strange ability to do this also with magnetic fields. While the effect is slight, in the presence of a magnet any given volume of the metal will change. This variation can be exploited in various ways starting with, of course, sensors that can detect fluctuations in magnetic fields.

Our own, (unsophisticated) tests have failed to change the volume of one of these cubes to the point that it can be measured with a caliper. For that, however, we’re actually thankful since for all we know if it did we’d have problems with terbium cubes bursting our plastic holders every time they came close to a magnet! We give each of these beautifully finished cubes the white glove treatment - and we’re sure you will too :-)

Title: Thulium Mirror Cube
Body HTML:
Pricey and exotic sounding, thulium’s 15 minutes of fame may never come. Without any highly desirable chemical traits and also its extreme difficulty in extraction keep this one firmly in the list of most obscure entries in the periodic table.

Given the high cost of production and that it belongs in the “rare earth element” family one might be excused for thinking that thulium is extremely rare in nature. It isn’t. The cost to make thulium metal is high primarily due to it being so similar to the other rare earths (which are invariably always found mixed with) that it takes complex and lengthy processes to isolate. That and, again, that there aren’t many buyers looking for it in the first place.

Now that Luciteria has been acquiring thulium by the kilogram it may well be the case that this newfound demand channel is sufficient to affect the price and availability of this sleepy commodity. And that would be great news as far as we’re concerned!

Within our premium line of density cubes, thulium was a late addition given some unique metallurgical characteristics that made the normal polishing techniques inadequate to achieve a mirror-like finish. It turns out that the ultrapure metal tends to have a rather coarse metal grain which “catches” in the grit of the polishing machine resulting in chips and gouges that ruin the job. Unfortunately, our supplier did not share technical details on how this was overcome but, at least, the problem was eventually solved though - of course - not without significant costs to research the solution…. all reflected in the rather steep price of ownership!

Title: Titanium Mirror Cube
Body HTML: Titanium, synonymous with all things hi-tech and luxury tier, is naturally strong as steel but only half the weight. Although few objects that boast of being made of titanium show off how beautiful the metal is in this offering we do just that. Made of the same 99.99% pure grade of titanium as the regular titanium cubes we sell, these have had a lot of extra polishing to bring out the shine in a big way!

Title: Tungsten Mirror Cube
Body HTML:
Tungsten is the most popular metal to be turned into cubes. This is in large part because its high density makes them irresistible as playthings. Now we turn up the desirability factor to ten on the dial with these extremely reflective versions. If you’re in the market for a tungsten cube as desktop bling then these are unquestionably the nicest ones you can get your hands on!

50mm (and larger) cubes can be specially ordered but note they take 3-4 months to deliver from time of placing the order.

Title: Vanadium Mirror Cube
Body HTML:
Vanadium is an exotic metal that plays a minor role in the steel making industry. It would play a much larger role if it wasn't so rare and expensive because it has many desirable properties.

These little 1cm cubes have been painstakingly polished to reveal a supremely reflective surface on all six sides and begs comparison to other similar cubes. It is one of the few metals that really is neutral, medium gray!

Title: Ytterbium Mirror Cube
Body HTML: This beautiful metal is practically unknown except for those who want to memorize the hundred-plus roster making up the periodic table. Of a light golden, almost brassy color, ytterbium is almost impossible to shape via machining into anything useful (assuming someone wanted to for some reason) because, like wood, it has a grainy texture which tends to “catch” onto tools and break away leaving a pitted surface. It is, for this reason, that it is nearly impossible to polish. Only with the greatest care, using fastidiously small increments in grit size and taking it reeeeeal slow can the luster be coaxed. Because of this it takes many days to make a single one of these cubes and, to be honest, the surface can only achieve a satin rather than true mirror finish. While technically possible, it would probably costs hundreds of dollars using the current tech to make a single gem-like cubed centimeter of ytterbium would surely cost hundreds of dollars. As it is, the satin finish is absolutely gorgeous and really brings out the character of this rare earth!

Title: Yttrium Mirror Cube
Body HTML:
These three little cubes are the first prototypes of yttrium passing the mirror polishing test - and it is almost certainly the first instance anyone has ever taken the trouble to polish this metal. Although not a lanthanide, yttrium is usually regarded as a rare earth metal because its properties fall right in line with the rest of the REEs and is also found mixed with those. It is, from the point of view of metallurgy, pretty much useless. It produces prodigious amounts of sparks when friction is applied turning it into a flammable hazard when machining. It is too rare and expensive to be used in its pure elemental form anyway.

The primary challenge in polishing yttrium to a very reflective luster is therefore to control speed of the polishing wheel in such a way that the cube never becomes heated. It is also susceptible, like most other rare earth metals, to rapid oxidation when moist so needs special dry grit abrasives. This high degree of pain-in-the-assness is responsible for the high cost.

Title: Zinc Mirror Cube
Body HTML:
Zinc is a metal that easily polishes but it’s also one that is rather environmentally fragile (oxidizes easily) and is also delicate to work with. This increases its difficulty in machining to this degree and is reflected in the price. On the other hand the incredibly smooth surfaces make it easy to appreciate its clearly bluegreen hue compared to other, grayer metals when side by side.

We suggest handling with gloves or at least quickly wiping down prints so as to avoid discoloration.

Title: Zirconium Mirror Cube
Body HTML: As normally polished, pure zirconium metal is a beautiful metal with a very light brownish tinge similar to titanium. The metal’s character truly shines (heh, pun) when polished to a mirror finish. This will be one you reach for when you want to show off a beautifully made cube!

Title: Tin Mirror Cube
Body HTML:
Tin is a metal that combines several desirable characteristics. It is cheap, non-toxic and resists corrosion. It has, unfortunately, the fatal flaw of being very soft which precludes it from being used in any type of structural application except as a coating.

Its softness makes it easy to polish into a mirror (in fact, this being one of its modern day uses) even though this softness also means that it can dent and scratch easily. You can buff out a hairline scratch but it’ll take great care to prevent dulling an edge. A little extra TLC should keep your six-sided mirror beautiful indefinitely.

Title: Mercury Metal "Cube"
Body HTML:
Depending on just how nitpicky you want to be, mercury is arguably the only metal that is a liquid at room temperature and unlike contenders cesium and gallium it most certainly is the only metal that stays liquid even in the type of room whose heating fails in the middle of a Minnesota winter. In fact, mercury’s most notable role through the last three centuries has been its use in thermometers as it reliably marks the hottest summer peaks and all but the coldest of polar winters. In this last use it, of course, has over the past couple of decades all but become as commonplace as phone booths for reasons of safety (at least insofar as public perceptions are concerned).

But therein lies the problem. Unless you happen to live in Siberia or Alaska the chances of it being cold enough for mercury to freeze (at around 38° below) your cube collection is never going to be complete. Worry ye not! Thanks to the amazing properties of nature we can fake our way through this little inconvenience by injecting the metal into a plastic container and sticking a cheap sticker on top and whoaa, heyyy, we’ve a mercury cube now! While we were initially skeptic they easily exceeded expectations as they look and feel great in hand.

Ps. disclaimer time: these cubes are, in case it needs to be spelled out, meant as scientific collectibles. The lids are glued shut to prevent leakage or noxious vapors from escaping but will probably not survive a fall onto a concrete floor nor present much of a challenge to a kid with a hammer. The mercury within poses no harm to you or anyone else so long as it stays inside the box. Breaking it out on purpose to play with the same way bored kids used to do with those old thermometers back in the 1970’s is a really really really bad idea. Cut ‘em a little slack… they didn’t have Xbox or TikTok, you know? So unless you’re 100% confident that you can keep this one away from the more buttered-fingered or mischievous members of your family then we seriously urge you to be okay with not filling this hole in your set.

New as of May 2024: we are discontinuing the old glued boxes filled with mercury and switching over to sealed glass. More expensive but a whole lot more elegant!

Title: Fluorine Cube 75%
Body HTML:
Wait, fluorine’s a gas, right? Yes, and an impossible one to capture under ordinary conditions. This gas is likely the most immediately lethal of any natural element on the Periodic Table. You would sooner do laps in a swimming pool filled with mercury or sunbathe in plutonium’s glow than take a single whiff of fluorine. It is, in fact, so chemically reactive that it can happily eat through glass which is among the most inert of solid materials.

And that poses a problem for collectors looking to fill the ninth slot on their display case. What to put in there? Teflon, the miracle invention that lets you scrape off last week’s Mac’n’Cheese from the pan with with a flick of the wrist, is a compound that is 75% fluorine and 25% carbon. The two atoms lock into long chains so tenaciously that it becomes both a physical and chemical barrier. Those leftovers have nothing to cling onto so slide right off.

While this is clearly the poor man’s alternative, 75% is actually remarkably rich for a compound. It is purer, comparatively, than sodium in salt (at only 40%) or even the amount of iron in rust (66%). Fluorite, the typical stand-in for this element in collections, is actually less than 50% fluorine. This makes Teflon a very credible substitute; at least until such day that we can fashion exotic cube-shaped quartz traps to hold the pure gas. Best of all, it’s pretty cheap!

An unforeseen hurdle in the manufacture of these cubes was the challenge of engraving them. Usually a simple process, charring via laser produces a nice contrast to make the cubes legible.
