In the form of these bars and given their modest price, delightful heft and rather attractive, dark luster we’ll do our best to promote higher awareness of this metal among the public.

Title: Tungsten .9995 1 Oz Bar
Body HTML:
China, the leading producer of just about everything, accounts for some 80% of the world’s output of tungsten. Because the metal is so hard most of it is used to make tools but new applications have appeared of late putting strain on suppliers to keep up. Two major events made a bad situation worse. First, in 2018 the Chinese version of the EPA (up until then an almost toothless government body) decided to come down hard on Baoji. This is one of China’s most heavily industrialized and polluted provinces. New restrictions were enacted overnight and implemented in a coordinated Blitzkrieg meant to capture the worst offenders red handed. The aftermath temporarily slowed the entire tungsten industry to a trickle with a resulting spike in the price of this metal. Then, a couple of years later, the pandemic’s lockdowns shuttered mills and refineries choking the supply again. Elsewhere in the world other restrictions left receiving ports clogged and decimated business purchase orders for a recovery that would stretch many painful months.

The one-two punch was hard on the world’s ‘tungstenomics’ but the strong bounceback in the industrialized world has nearly restored trade levels to its former levels. The bullion industry now opens a new page with the understated introduction of these bars and may well turn into a significant demand channel for this metal in the years to come.

Title: Rhodium .9995 1 oz Bar
Body HTML:
Save for the infamous tulips craze of the 1600’s, never in the history of commerce has a commodity’s value pulled so far ahead of that of its peers as in the case of rhodium. While ever a pricey member of the platinum group, rhodium’s poor familiarity in the public kept it in the background and for years its price hovered in the low hundreds per ounce. For reasons not entirely known, the onset of the global Covid pandemic unleashed the horse within and rhodium’s spot price rocketed as if it had been the latest crypto meme coin. In January of 2021 it reached a heady $29,000 per ounce; over fifteen times the price of a similar bar of gold!

In the months since the rare metal’s price has pulled back some but still remains many times more the price of perennial bullion favorites gold, platinum and palladium. Unlike the case with these three, however, rhodium truly is breathtakingly rare in nature and there’s nary an ounce stockpiled anywhere. Barring some economic catastrophe the last thing you should expect is for rhodium to come back down in price to some former, more reasonable level. By every expectation its rarity and covetability will keep its price unfortunately out of reach to all but well-heeled investors.

With the launch of this bar, Luciteria now joins a very short list of companies that offer stamped rhodium bullion and in doing so we extend our lead to the most comprehensive list of pure metals in this format. Ships by armored truck to your home or office ;-)

Payment for this item qualifies for a 2% discount if paid via bank wire transfer. Please inquire by email prior to purchase.

Title: Thulium .9999 1 Gram Bar
Body HTML:
Although lutetium is (marginally) the rarest of the rare earth metals, thulium not only comes a second close but is also considerably more expensive. The reason why isn’t entirely clear. The Chinese refineries are remarkably tight-lipped about when it comes to divulging anything having to do with operations or sales data not just to their western customers but even to their Chinese colleagues as they’re all afraid information can be used to the market advantage of their competitors. In any case, both lutetium and thulium are metals with very low demand atop of their being so rare. Or perhaps due to it. Thulium has the added burden of being a finicky metal that is hard to work with as it very much tends to fall apart during tooling. The white glove treatment, of course, adds to the cost of manufacture.

Measures 15x8x0.94mm

Title: Magnesium .999 1 Gram Bar
Body HTML:
Keep this one dry. Unlike the case with most other metals, magnesium is not easily susceptible to oxygen. Under ambient temperatures it is relatively inert and plays nice with the other metals in your collection. Its Achille’s heel is high levels of humidity which dull the luster of the metal covering it in an ugly haze that can’t be restored unless physically scraped off.

As magnesium is one of the lightest metals this one gram bar looks a bit like a horse pill. For best effect, make sure to pick up a tungsten one of the same weight!

Measures 15x8x5.04mm

Title: Bismuth .9999 1 oz Bar
Body HTML:
Apart from their “silvery” color and similar density, bismuth and silver share one other trait: they’re both about equal in terms of rarity. That bismuth is considerably cheaper is all due to lower demand. It is, essentially, a fake metal. Despite its luster and heft, bend it - even just a little - and bismuth breaks rather than gives. And it is delicate as glass. This alone rules it out for most practical uses.

Still, it has a lot going for it. It forms stunning, surrealistic multihued crystals that are sold as ornaments and even though it’s considered a ‘heavy metal’ it is completely nontoxic. On exposure to humid air, and especially high heat, the metal becomes attractively tarnished in all the colors of the rainbow. Those beautiful colors are, unfortunately, hard to control as the punchiest tones are achieved close to the melting point of the element.

Title: Platinum .999 1 oz Bar
Body HTML:
Platinum is the third most popularly traded bullion metal after gold and silver. It needs little by way of introduction to investors. Each year, on average, the combined worldwide mining output adds a paltry 180 tons of new metal to the supply chain - a figure equivalent to a train engine or a typical American house. Of this amount nearly half is immediately shunted to the automotive industry where it’s put to the unglamorous use of being turned into catalytic converters. A quarter goes to the much more glamorous jewelry sector and the last quarter is divvied up into at least a half dozen major channels like chemical manufacturing, electronics, dentistry and, of course, into the making of bars and coins. The list of countries having at least dabbled in platinum coinage probably runs into the dozens as it holds the top slot for prestige. That it currently trails gold in value is a historical anomaly which is likely to self correct in the coming years given the demand from so many sectors and, most importantly, the fact that its production rate is just a tenth that of gold.

Payment for this item must be via bank wire transfer. Established Luciteria customers exempted. Please inquire via email.

Title: Chromium .999 1 oz Bar
Body HTML:
Chromium’s claim to fame is that it is both very hard and rust proof under normal ambient conditions. For this reason it’s the magic ingredient that turns ordinary steel into stainless steel. Mother Nature has blessed this planet with bountiful chrome so miners each year pull millions of tons of it to be used in, well, basically everything that you see around you that has shiny metal bits to it.

Like diamond, chromium’s hardness comes at the cost of brittleness. When pure it is of little use besides spraying onto cheap hubcaps because it cracks and crumbles with little effort. The making of solid bars with raised relief was a considerable headache for the engineers who have made the rest of these bars; explaining why it was the rather late 41st such bar to be added to the lineup.

Title: Chromium .999 1 Gram Bar
Body HTML:
Chromium often gets a bad name unfairly. Hexavalent chromium, often shortened to Cr(VI), is an especially serious industrial pollutant and carcinogenic. However, as often spoken about on TV and other mass media reporting, confusion often arises because it makes it seem like the element itself is at fault rather than pointing out that the culprit are derivative compounds and not the metal itself. While the focus is on the toxic effluents of poorly regulated factories there’s unfortunately little to no effort expended in pointing out that the average household already contains many pounds of this element from your dishwasher to your faucets, doorknobs, phone and virtually every other identifiably metallic thing you come across day to day; all of which we very much hope is harmless or we’re surely doomed as a steel-free world is unthinkable in a modern world.

Also totally harmless are these well-made little planchets of pure chromium metal. One of the very few instances of seeing products manufactured from the pure form of this element without the iron with which it is nearly always alloyed.

Title: Lead .999 1 Gram Bar
Body HTML:
Safe to say that exactly zero businesses in history have made a business plan around selling lead as bullion, true, but history does have a very long track of mines making good coin from it all the same. Whether to make pipes, cannon balls or even the lowly fisherman’s sinkers lead has been there as an affordable commodity that is easy to shape and build with.

While relatively light gray when freshly molten, lead becomes the darkest of metals soon after with tarnish from exposure to air. Unlike other types of corrosion, however, that tarnish acts as a protective coat to prevent damage below the surface. Maligned as a poison, its danger comes primariily from small dissolved amounts in food and water rather than direct touch.

Title: Iridium .9995 1 oz Bar
Body HTML:
Iridium is in many ways the ultimate precious metal. Extremely dense, super rare, rustproof and very, very costly. With that enviable resume how come it’s not as popular as gold or platinum? Well, its Achille’s heel is that it’s damn near impossible to make anything out of it. With a melting point twice the maximum operating temperature that ordinary furnaces can reach, casting is out of the question. It cannot be hammered into shape as doing so will make it shatter. It can’t be cut, lathed, drilled, milled or tooled in any way for it invariably breaks both itself and the machine that dared to form it.

So this otherwise highly desirable noble metal is put to use in decidedly blue collar tasks such as turning it into spark plug tips, catalytic converters and even crucibles to hold lesser metals in their molten state. Despite these background roles investors have long wanted to add iridium to their portfolios given the attractive proposition of its great rarity on the one hand and the rapidly growing number of its potential uses on the other.

Luciteria is immensely proud to now offer these beautifully made bars of essentially pure iridium ahead of the leading bullion makers which have for a variety of reasons not been able to produce them so far. Quantities are very limited because of the great difficulty in making them as well as securing the raw material itself.

INTERNATIONAL CUSTOMERS MUST SELECT FEDEX/DHL/UPS FOR SHIPPING.

Payment for this item qualifies for a 2% discount if paid via bank wire transfer. Please inquire by email prior to purchase.

Title: Germanium .99999 1 oz Bar
Body HTML:
While the chemistry world was aware of this rare element as far back as the 19th century, it wasn’t until the birth of electronics in the mid 20th century that a use for it was found. A semiconductor like silicon, it has been employed in this industry ever since. However, in most applications silicon offers better performance and, given the fact that it costs nearly 1,000 times less than germanium, the use of this latter is a trickle in comparison.

Optics is another field that requires a constant supply of germanium for the manufacture of night vision goggles, photography lenses and similar hi-tech uses.

Because this metalloid is as fragile as glass it can’t be made by die striking so the lettering here is laser engraved rather than physical relief. However, the material can be polished to extreme reflectivity and, along with its pale yellow-green color, the combination makes this perhaps the most beautiful of all the bars we carry.

Title: Boron .999 1 oz Bar
Body HTML:
Boron, mysterious boron, is an element that hides under plain sight and is far rarer than one might suspect given the ubiquity of its compounds: borax, boric acid and borosilicate glass being three well known applications. The important thing to know right up front is that even though the element is common enough the pure element isn’t at all. This is because isolating boron from the chemical bonds it creates is an arduous task involving costly, time-consuming steps made all the costlier by the fact that in the end it’s pretty useless in this uncombined state.

Partly to blame: pure boron is exceptiionally hard and brittle much like its cousin carbon. Unlike carbon however crystalline boron does not make for mouth watering gems so very few go through all that bother. If it were merely brittle like glass that wouldn’t be so bad. Adding to thiis material’s list of difficulties we find that it has a very high meltinig point so it can’t be melted (like glass) into a mold by which we could obtain a desired shape. One must first make a big lump of it, chemically or via specialized furnaces, and then carve from that the form that is sought. But boron throws one last curveball in that it is so hard that even doing this otherwise straightforward goal requires a diamond saw!

Luciiteria is happy to add boron as its newest option in the bullion ounce series. We do regret that the steep cost will keep this out of the hands of most collectors.

Title: Silicon .999999 1 oz Bar
Body HTML: Practically synonymous with all things high-tech, silicon enables our modern life. Without it we would still be in the Industrial Age. Fortunately, the world has plenty of it. Beaches are essentially rusted silicon that when cleaned up turns to glass and, when de-oxidized, looks like this bar. While there is no shortage of the stuff, the amount of processing it takes to purify it to the levels necessary for turning it into microchips is so costly - and demand is so strong - that the we’re left with a perennial bottleneck that keeps prices far above what the abundance of the raw material suggests.

Title: Ruthenium .9995 1 Gram Bar
Body HTML:
The investment world took notice of palladium some 20 years ago and the result was a steady increase in value that often far outstripped the growth of more traditional metals like silver, gold and platinum. Ruthenium, a much less well known member of the so-called platinum group of metals, has in the last few years has been quietly but steadily rising in price despite pretty much zero attention from investors. If palladium’s rise in price was at least in great part due to new demand coming from the bullion markets how is this metal doing it while “flying under the radar”?

One likely answer is that the traditional uses for platinum, and then palladium, was from automakers who used significant quantities of these precious metals in the making of catalytic converters. As the prices of these rose over the years these automakers have switched to ruthenium as a cheaper alternative and are perhaps stocking up in expectation of future shortages.

While there are no accurate figures, the best estimates peg annual production at around 30 tons or less. That’s a paltry figure compared to the 200 tons or so of platinum and palladium. So what happens when the investment world does take notice of ruthenium given that it’s nearly 10 times rarer but still somehow priced at half the price of the others? Just a mere blip from a hedge fund manager is all it will take to send the price skyward.

We’re happy to bring to the public the first handful of one gram bars of this very promising metal. Whether you’re an investor, an element collector or just interested in unusual offerings we’re convinced that there will never be a better opportunity to own this rare a commodity at this low a price.

Title: Rhenium
Body HTML:
According to Wikipedia rhenium holds the title of rarest of the stable, naturally occurring metals with a concentration of only 0.7 parts per billion in the earth's crust. That's six times rarer than gold! This precious metal is very resistant to corrosion and has a very high melting point making it perfect for many applications.... if only it wasn't so rare and expensive! NASA and the Pentagon though have generous budgets which is why they can afford to use a large portion of all the tiny amount of rhenium mined every year to go into jet engines and rockets. Claim your little bit 'o the stuff for your collection before the Gov hogs up every last piece of it :-)

A one gram bead (or two half gram beads) of pure rhenium goes into each round container.

Title: Tellurium
Body HTML:
Tellurium is one of the rarest stable elements outside of the precious metals. Small amounts are used in electronics due to its unusual electrical properties…. but has few other uses leaving it in perennial obscurity.

For your collection we get you a beautiful chip of very, very pure tellurium. 99.99999% to be sure!

Title: Antimony
Body HTML: A metalloid. A not quite metallic enough metal. They sure *look* like metal.... except when you drop it and it shatters like glass! Antimony would be one good example of this (though it's not quite as fragile as glass). The element however crumbles easily and is a skin irritant. All the more reason to appreciate its intricate crystalline structure in this handy container containing little pieces of 99.9999% pure Sb.

Title: Rhodium .9995 1 Gram Bar
Body HTML:
Rhodium is now, and will likely continue to be, the most expensive precious metal you can buy by an exponential margin.

This wasn’t the case historically. Up until the mid-2010s this was an obscure member of the platinum group with little trade that tracked a deadbeat line year on year across the historical price charts. Things started changing when automobile execs, looking for new alternatives to platinum and palladium towards the making of catalytic converters, decided to ask for it. Unlike platinum and palladium, however, rhodium truly is a rare commodity. The mines that deal in these metals quickly tapped out of their reserves. Then came Covid and the {expletive} hit the fan. Rhodium’s price spiked to an unbelievable $30,000 an ounce - easily worth more than stack of a dozen ounces of gold or platinum.

Those automakers have since eased off the gas on buying rhodium (what’s the point?) and its price has come down but still remains comically high. There’s still a vacuum and those mines are probably still trying to fill backlogged orders but everyone is coming to terms with the fact that the earth is stingy and yields but a sip of this precious liquid.

We are not the first company to bring rhodium bullion to market. That honor goes to the Cohen mint, a now defunct business, that experimented coining in this metal and whose products are still traded and sought after to this day. So, in this case, we’re happy to be the second company to offer bullion rhodium by the gram!

INTERNATIONAL CUSTOMERS MUST SELECT FEDEX/DHL/UPS FOR SHIPPING.

Title: Manganese .997 1 oz Bar
Body HTML:
Manganese breaks an unusual type of record. In the whole roster of metals none beats this element when it comes to the difference between the price it’s sold at and its actual value as a commodity. It is so cheap that its spot price is measured not in ounces or even kilograms but by the ton. It is a tenth the cost of nickel, another industrial product with similar uses. Coming up with an intrinsic value for this ounce is more or less like trying to calculate the value of a paper clip. Clearly, the price listed for this bar has to be an epic typo, right? Nope.

Manganese refineries deliver a product that looks a little like potato chips. The flakes are shiny on one side and black and mottled on the other. And the list of things you can do with it is basically one: to toss it into a furnace with molten iron to make steel. If you try to melt and cast into a mould it will turn to a messy black lump of oxides well before it actually melts. If you try hammering it into shape it will shatter like glass. Cutting, polishing, lathing or any other type of physical action on pure manganese metal yields nothing but broken ugly bits of less pure manganese.

What’s going on here? While perfectly stable, non-toxic and a seemingly ordinary metal at room temperature, manganese has the singular misfortune of being both extremely brittle and susceptible to oxidation at high temperatures. Many metals, of course, will oxidize easily but none compare to manganese’s dual personality of being practically inert at ambient temps then almost explosively combustible a couple hundred degrees hotter.

This by itself would not be an insurmountable challenge. Metals that oxidize easily can be molten and cast in furnaces that have had the air pumped out and replaced with argon, for example. And, in effect, this is also how manganese is prepared initially as the molten metal forms a thin sheet on the crucible as it cools. However, as noted above, the sheet darkens on one side and shatters the second it is exposed to air. The darkening is as a result of the air’s oxygen attacking the still warm surface facing out and the shattering from simply being scraped off the surface. Using the same casting method it can also be molten into a lump rather than a sheet but as it cools the mass will contract and develop many internal cracks. It basically turns into something like popcorn (you can see this oxide-free variant sold here). Whichever route you take, however, the bottom line is that you simply cannot get pure manganese to form into an intended shape.

Making purposeful shapes of manganese requires a completely different process called sintering, which will be familiar to buyers of tungsten and some of the precious metals. Sintering begins with a chemical process that yields the pure metal in powder form. Under great pressure and heat the powder forms a mass that is outwardly solid but is internally quite weak and spongelike as it’s riddled with voids where the microscopic particles don’t fully touch. All sintering is good for is getting the basic shape down and compact enough that it won’t fall apart at a mere touch like a sand castle. But while tungsten and other metals yield a final or near-finished piece from the sintering process, manganese still requires extra steps. This is because the powder has to be pressed in anoxic chambers and then allowed to fully cool before bringing out into the open. And while sintered tungsten can be worked on without fear of ruining the piece the manganese blanks can’t as the friction of tooling or polishing risk both the feared oxidation as well as new cracks forming from stress. It has to be babied from start to finish including the final step of laser engraving.

In the end, Luciteria was able to commission the making of just fourteen bars at the cost of $400 a pop and a wait time of nearly two years from the first technical discussions. This is why a buck’s worth of metal ends up costing several hundred times more.

Title: Iridium .9995 1 Gram Bar
Body HTML:
Iridium cedes the crown of densest of all metals to its neighbor on the periodic table, osmium, which edges it out by just 1/1000th of a percent - effectively a tie. The two are also in a statistical dead heat in terms of that other crown; the rarest of all stable precious metals. Unlike osmium’s as-yet tepid demand and name recognition, however, iridium takes a clear lead with a slew of uses in various hi-tech applications. All of these strain the meager annual output which is estimated at just 5-10 tons per annum.

With this situation it is not surprising to find that the price of the metal to be high. While it was never cheap, the spot price quadrupled when Covid hit and has been hovering at around $6,000/oz ever since. For it to retreat to its pre-pandemic trading ranges all those traditional industrial suppliers would need to pare back their buy orders and hope that the nascent bullion and jewelry interest in this metal fizzles out.

Fat chance.

INTERNATIONAL CUSTOMERS MUST SELECT FEDEX/DHL/UPS FOR SHIPPING.

Title: Sulfur .9999 1 Gram Bar
Body HTML:
A big bag of this stuff can be picked up at a gardening store for a few bucks but a piece that fits on a pinky sets you back a cool twenty. What gives?

Known to everyone’s who’s ever smelled a really bad fart, sulfur is pretty much everywhere. Combined with hydrogen in your guts or in rotting eggs it gives off one of the most unpleasant stinks known to humans. And, the point here before we get sidetracked too much, is that it’s found pretty much everywhere. Bullion is supposed to be for materials that are rare and valuable. Who in their right mind would pay ten times more for this worthless stuff than a similar amount of silver? Not many. But, again, why is it so expensive in bar form?

Because sulfur is a powdery mess. It melts easily but also burns just as easily. Due to its brittleness when you cast it it tends to break apart or incorporate unwanted bubbles or other imperfections. It crystallizes in unexpected ways ruining a large portion of the half-unfinished pieces. It drives our techs nuts. In short, you’re mostly paying for the trouble it takes to make the damn thing behave as though it were an ordinary building material. Google the phrase “made of pure sulfur” and you’ll get llistings for… this page.

Few, it seems, have ever bothered making objects out of this substance be it for the bother or for the pointlessness. Well, now at least you know your money will be going towards a bit of trailblazing in the world of materials engineering. Short of that, there’s always the alternative of swallowing it and turning it into a foul smelling cloud with which to amuse your friends for… science.

Title: Bismuth .9999 1 Gram Bar
Body HTML:
Although every bit as rare in nature as silver, bismuth trades at just a fraction of its cost. We’ve seen though how these value relationships are rarely ever linear. Rarity, after all, is only half of the equation. The other side - and the more important of the two commercially - is demand. Bismuth is both fairly rare and fairly… unwanted. There’s a long list of industrially important chemicas that include bismuth, and an equally lengthy list of metallurgical concoctions that need it too, but none of it adds up to a demand so strong that it puts any sort of real squeeze on supply. It doesn’t help that its main output comes from lead and copper mining, two of the most intensively exploited metals worldwide. Meaning that any potential blip upwards in terms of demand could be met with a flood of new bismuth coming from those mines if the opportunity ever presented itself.

Bismuth is not a full member of the metals family. Science places it on the fence line between metals and non-metals. It certainly looks metallic, yes, but it’s much too soft and brittle to do anything with. These two properties make it a challenge to form into bars, thus accounting for both the price and the scarce availability of these bullion bars.

Title: Gram Bar Display Case
Body HTML:
New for 2023, your 1-gram bars now have an attractive home so you can show them off and keep them safe. 55 slots cover each of the bars we’ve made so far, all a perfect and snug fit. It’s a two part set with a removable front panel that is easy to place securely by means of corner magnets. Also comes with a little stand.

The display case measures 75x30mm (7.5” x 5.5”)

Title: Manganese
Body HTML:
An industrial metal, manganese is widely used to render desirable properties to steel such as strength and resistance to corrosion. However, it is much less commonly reduced to a pure state as it's also finicky to work with in this form. In particular, at high temperatures the metal is notoriously sensitive to oxidation. The very slightest trace of oxygen can spoil acres of its surface making for especially difficult procedures if one were to seek pristine surfaces.

Gleaming white manganese is hard to come by but we go out of our way to find the most attractive types :-)

Title: Cadmium
Body HTML:
Cadmium. They used to make rechargeable batteries from this stuff, remember? Its fifteen years of fame ended when lithium came to town and now this poor metal is back in obscurity.

Love it, hate it or be indifferent but here it is all the same: a chunk of cadmium in an all-transparent round plastic container to fill out your set!

Title: Lead
Body HTML: Ok, let's be honest. Lead might be just a couple of protons away from gold but it's at the other end of the scale in desirability and its humility is reflected in its traditional uses. Fish sinkers, bullets and bathroom pipes. When setting out to complete a collection of all the elements you can "upgrade" to a higher level of purity than the commercial grade - and prices quickly go up to unrealistic levels when you reach into the 5- and 6N's - but here we offer only the “regular” type of lead rated at 99.9% purity at a price anyone can afford!

Title: Calcium
Body HTML:
Even though you get it from drinking milk and yogurt people shouldn't be surprised to learn that calcium is a metal. After all, reading through the ingredients list on a cereal box there's iron and magnesium and others too. But the thing is that calcium isn't something most are familiar with as an object.

And yet, it really is a metal. On the moon it would make for a perfect metal to make, I don't know, a cell tower or something. It's very light and plentiful. Why the moon? Well, because there's no atmosphere or water there that could come in contact with our imaginary calcium tower. Either of those touch it and the calcium instantly sizzles and corrodes.

So keeping it in metallic form here on earth takes a little work. We've put this piece in a glass ampule with argon gass to do the trick.

Title: Arsenic
Body HTML:
This infamous element has the dubious credit of being the tool by which who knows how many people were murdered. While the pure element as shown isn’t highly toxic its oxides sure are. A little bit in food goes unnoticed and over the course of days or weeks the victim’s health declines until they have just enough strength to sign over a last will and testament to the merriment of the poisoner. It was a death that back in the old day, before blood lab tests and autopsies, left few clues as to what did in the poor sap.

Once autopsies and blood tests came into use during the late 19th century and into the 20th arsenic’s role in killing people gave way to its use as a poison of vermin but this, too, was eventually phased out in favor of other chemicals. The 21st century has resuscitated arsenic for use in the making of gallium arsenide transistors.

It is this last for which high purity arsenic is refined today in electronics laboratories. And for you we’ll pick a big, beautiful crystal for your collection. Well, if you have a liberal interpretation of beauty at least.

Title: Beryllium
Body HTML: Element number 4 on the periodic table happens to be the lightest structural metal and it would be an absolutely fantastic option if it wasn't so outrageously rare and expensive. A few years ago a hi-tech firm took the trouble to make a bike frame out of beryllium which cost an astounding $25,000 each. In solid form the metal is lighter than aluminum but much harder and stiffer. It would be possible to make a skyscraper ten times taller than the Empire State if it was made of Be. The metal however is toxic when shaved down to dust as it lodges in the lungs causing a disease that thankfully very few people need to worry about since, again, the metal is too rare and valuable to be used in anything outside of very specialized applications.

Title: Magnesium
Body HTML:
Magnesium doesn't pose much of a challenge even if it's not something you can find at Home Depot. Widely used in industry as an alloying agent and also a lab staple for a wide variety of chemistry applications, it is most often sold as ribbons or powdered metal.

For this offer we’ve chosen an attractive and blindingly white piece of magnesium metal to fill the spot at number 12 on the ol’ table.

Title: Strontium
Body HTML:
This rarely seen metal is most typically associated with nuclear pollution. Finding the strontium-90 isotope in the atmosphere around your neighborhood is bad news indeed and should be cause for immediate plans to relocate to your mother-in-law's for a while. But don't worry, that should only happen if you live near a malfunctioning nuclear power plant.

Strontium's other isotopes are considerably less harmful. In fact, one big use of it goes into toothpaste because it coats teeth in a microscopic film that protects against cavities. The piece here would be enough to seal a city's worth of its denizen's teeth. We really don't recommend chewing on it though.

Title: Titanium
Body HTML: Titanium's growing popularity is due to excellent properties. Light, strong and corrosion resistant, titanium is the luxury version of steel. These days anything made of metal that wants to sound high end and high tech will sport titanium in the branding. From tiny screws to entire submarines have been made of titanium!

Title: Zinc
Body HTML:
Whenever you see the word "galvanized" in relation to anything metal that's zinc they're talking about. Galvanizing (coating in zinc) is done to give an oxidation-prone metal a little more resistance to the elements. Which is ironic because zinc is a rather fragile metal in said elements. Pure zinc is a dazzling metal with a very faint blue-green hue. That beautiful luster slowly dulls in air until it's all gone and the zinc has turned a dirty looking gray.

A small piece weird-shaped piece similar to the one pictured will come with your container and, being airtight, should keep it shiny for the long term!

Title: Samarium
Body HTML: On the lanthanides row of the periodic table samarium is the first metal that doesn't rust in plain old air. It also happens to be one of the most of the series though that hardly means you can buy cutlery made of the stuff. In fact, about the only thing where samarium is widely used is as flints in cigarette lighters. This exploits one of the traits of the rare earths which is that they create showers of sparks when struck by another metal as small, overheated particles rapidly oxidize.

Title: Barium
Body HTML:
Barium is one of the rarer metals to acquire for the collector. The main problem stems from its very reactive nature that makes it very difficult to keep it from oxidizing. The difficulty in reducing the metal from the ores as well as the lack of a substantial market for barium in elemental form make difficult to acquire and expensive.

As is the case with the rest of the reactive metals on offer in these acrylic boxes, we embed the samples in a little vial immune to the ravages of air’s oxygen!

Title: Ytterbium
Body HTML:
The town of Ytterby near Stockholm, Sweden, yielded the chemistry world with ores rich in mysterious new elements. Named after its namesake town, ytterbium is one of the rare earth metals misnamed for not actually being all that rare but infamous for the tremendous difficulty needed to separate it from its neighbors in this group. 

For no reason other than its weird name, ytterbium is a popular first choice for those looking to expand their collection into the mysterious rare earths group. Here we include a few grams of this slightly yellow and exotic metal.

Title: Europium
Body HTML:
Together with lutetium, europium is a very rare element - perhaps not in the absolute sense as it is comparatively abundant but certainly in the commercial sense where far fewer kilograms of europium come on the market any given year than truly rare metals like gold or platinum.

Much of the reason for this is that europium quickly oxidizes in air turning it from a metal into, well, an earthy type of material of little obvious use. I say “obvious” because this metal still does have plenty of uses; in electronics particularly.

In this offering we keep it lustrous with the aid of inert argon gas which will not chemically react with this or any other metal.

Title: Indium
Body HTML:
Germanium is named after Germany and francium after France but indium is not named after India. The name was instead inspired from spectroscopy where its discoverer analyzed a sample containing this metal gave off an indigo glow, a beautiful color halfway between blue and purple.

Indium is quite rare and expensive and is unusual for being so soft that you can knead it with your fingers!

Title: Thallium
Body HTML:
Thallium kills. It's an order of magnitude more poisonous than arsenic and just 3 grams of the stuff, less than the weight of a penny, is all that it takes to kill a person. But behind this bullet-proof window called Lucite the little sample within is safe enough that a baby can play with it. Not that that's a good idea, mind you! In addition to being poisonous, thallium is also a finicky metal that is difficult to keep free of surface oxides, becoming leaden gray under oil and degrading to dust in open air. 

Tucked in this plastic container is a small piece of pure thallium kept free of oxides thanks to being encapsulated in a small glass ampule with argon gas.

Title: Thulium
Body HTML:
This obscure metal is little more than a lab curiosity. Rare, expensive and difficult to separate from the other lanthanides, along with the fact it has few extraordinary properties, means thulium is destined to remain in obscurity. As the number of collectors of elements grow we wonder how long it will be before the majority of the demand simply comes from those wanting to own a little of this exotic metal!

These fragments of thulium come in the wonderfully textured form of the metal known in the trade as "dendritic" which is a fancy word for the branch-like shapes metals form when allowed to crystallize.

Title: Germanium
Body HTML:
Germanium is the only metal (or metalloid to be exact) that looks green. Just a slight tinge mind you, a yellow-green highlight when you look at it just right, but definitely there when you compare it side by side to your typical gray looking ordinary metal. It's a fetching sight to see a rock shaped object that is as reflective as the most well-polished mirror.

Here we offer pure germanium “rocks” in an attractive screw-top round container. The piece shown weighs five grams which nearly fills the container.

Title: Iodine
Body HTML:
For the collector of elements from the periodic table we find iodine is perhaps the most beautiful specimen as it comes in many attractive colors and forms. This element which we normally think of as a brown disinfectant can appear in various shades of amber, purple and magenta when dissolved and as black crystals in solid form. A sprinkling of pure iodine diffused in a non-polar solution becomes anywhere from light pink to deep purple while in a polar solution, such as in the gel offered here, turns a dramatic shade of amber.

Iodine is a tough element to place in a collection. Unless tightly sealed in a material that the element won’t dissolve (as iodine is very chemically reactive) it will soon stain everything around it. To make it behave we offer here tiny crystals trapped in glass. Opt for the cheaper solution with loose spheres but keep an eye on your sample that it’s not staining things nearby!

Title: Gallium
Body HTML: In the last few years gallium has become the plaything of nerds of all ages thanks to increased availability via novelty shops and YouTube videos. Its lack of toxicity and relative low cost help things further, turning it into a safe alternative to mercury which has been all but banned. A fascinating characteristic of gallium, apart from its body temperature melting point, is that when it solidifies it expands like ice does so storing it in glass is inadvisable.

Title: Nickel
Body HTML: We're all familiar with nickel because it's given its name to our 5-cent coin even though in that case it's really mostly copper. The name is better suited for Canadian nickels which were, in fact, made of 99.9% pure nickel for many years (but no longer). It is also always present in small amounts in ordinary steels and figures prominently in batteries and the manufacture of magnets. The sample here is of very high purity meant for use in the electronics industry where impurities become a much bigger issue.

Title: Vanadium
Body HTML: Exotic-sounding vanadium's 15 minutes of fame never came being eternally tasked as an alloying agent in steel. Very seldom has any lab bothered to reduce the compounds it's normally found in to its native state but, and here's where that fame might be warranted, vanadium may well top the list of rarest element found in its native state when a few specks of the isolated metal were discovered on the side of a Mexican volcano. You go vanadium! In the meantime, here's a little fruit of that rare lab effort when someone took the trouble to see what the metal actually looks like in pure form. And so attractive too these little leaf-shaped sparkling crystals.

Title: Sulfur
Body HTML:
Sulfur. Vaguely hellish and certainly associated with all things hot and stinky, this powdery element can sometimes be bought in drug stores and is often typo'd as "sulphur". On rare occasion it is found in nature as a translucent yellow crystal but more often as powder stain near volcanoes. It is an essential ingredient in the making of rubber and is infamous for that awful smell of rotten eggs - which is actually the deadly gas hydrogen sulfide!

Much purer than the store-bought kind, this 99.99% sulfur has been specially prepared in a laboratory into beautiful small crystals that are translucent. Easily one of the most beautiful natural elements!

Title: Neodymium
Body HTML:
Neodymium magnets have made this otherwise obscure element popular and these are found in many devices today. Even though the name might make it sound that the magnets are neodymium they're really only a minor component in otherwise ordinary iron magnets but the Nd is the secret sauce that turns it into a much stronger magnet.

Pure neodymium is a delicate metal that goes from shiny to tarnished in minutes and left out in air it will completely corrode in a matter of days into a pile of powdery dust. For this reason keeping it shiny requires encapsulation in glass with argon gas.

Title: Silicon
Body HTML:
Silicon is one of the most common elements found on earth. Sand, for one, is nothing but rusted silicon. You can't find it in its elemental form in nature because at the heated temperatures found in the primordial earth all of it oxidized or bound to other elements. Yet, when it is purified it is fairly inert and will stay that way even in moist, salty air.

Our silicon comes from the large rods, or boules, that get sliced up into wafers to be etched for microchips. At 99.9999999% pure it is the purest grade of any element that is commercially available. These chips are a gorgeous, reflective dark blue-gray and we're sure you'll love your little sample!

Title: Praseodymium
Body HTML:
When a talk show host wants to drive home the point that there are some very rare metals in your cell phone he might be tempted to give praseodymium as the leading example. After all, it appears nearly unpronounceable and would be familiar only to those who work with rare earth metals!

In actuality this timid metal isn't all that rare. The dirt you walk on has plenty. But, and there's always a but, getting the metal isolated from its earthy prison is not a trivial task. Also, keeping it from turning right back into "earth" is equally difficult. This is why it's offered here inside of a little glass vial which protects it from coming into contact with air or other things that prevent it from, you know, turning into that dirt we speak of!

Title: Lanthanum
Body HTML:
Lanthanum gives its name to a whole row on the period table, the lanthanides. Apart from this the little-known element is famous for little else. The metal is soft and rapidly corrodes if left out in the open.

The specimen provided here was carefully prepared in an all-glass enclosure with argon to keep it pure and bright.

Title: Cerium
Body HTML:
The rare earths aren't really all that rare. While they're widely distributed all over the earth they're rare enough in the sense that there aren't that many locations where you can find them in concentrated deposits that make mining profitable. And when you do find those deposits the refining steps yield misch metal which is sort of an onomatopoeia for what it actually is: a mishmash of seventeen different metals. Getting those isolated from each other has been rated as one of the major chemical engineering headaches of metallurgical science! In any case, cerium will account for about half of the misch metal alloy and is the most abundant of those 17 elements.

Our cerium sample comes in a little argon-filled ampule to protect it from oxidation and weighs approximately one gram.

Title: Dysprosium
Body HTML:
Like the other metals in the rare earths group, dysprosium is usually separated from its kin via time consuming processes that drive up the cost of getting the isolated element. Also as in the rest of these cases, the type that is most often available will be fibrous-looking pieces that form as crystals precipitated out of solution - and it's the end stage of the lab purification process before being shipped off to the customer.

As shown in this photo, this piece of dysprosium weighs five grams.

Title: Tin
Body HTML: Sure, tin isn't the sexiest metal but it's harder to find than you might think given that "tin cans" are really made from steel. Actually, very few household items contain much of this metal nowadays. This is a sample of ultrapure 99.99% electrolytically refined tin metal casting sputters attractively packaged in the round plastic container as shown; ready for a display case or on its own!

Title: Boron
Body HTML: Mysterious boron comes in two allotropes; a brown powder that looks rather identical to something you'd scrape off your shoe and a glassy type that looks like miniature asteroids. Despite being common, boron has a reputation for being fiendishly difficult to isolate into elemental form. That makes owning one of these specimens all the more special of course!

Title: Scandium
Body HTML: Scandium would be the perfect building metal if only it wasn't so damn rare! Light as aluminum but as strong as steel, scandium out-titaniums titanium as an exotic ingredient in high-tech doodads of the aerospace industry. China, Russia and Australia are the only countries where it is mined but of these only China currently refines it to its pure, metallic form. Our supplier, one of China’s leading exporters, boasts an annual production of less than 400 kilograms. That’s about the weight of a piano.

Title: Bismuth
Body HTML:
Many an element collector started out by coming across these little souvenirs bought at museum gift stores or rock shops. The weird Escher-like formations happen when molten bismuth begins to solidify at an uneven pace along its surfaces. As the surfaces collide they change directions at right angles according to its lattice structure and in so doing create the odd-looking walls and stair steps. The iridescence is due to very thin layers of oxidation and are allowed to occur for purely aesthetic reasons; a little thicker here, a little thinner there in each case a different color is produced due to light refraction.

A beautiful crystal will be included in your container weighing between 5-10 grams.

Title: Molybdenum
Body HTML:
Molybdenum may not be well known in the collective mind of the public but figures prominently in the world of metallurgy as one of the ingredients that, together with iron, makes steel. "Moly steel" is a collective term used for many steel grades containing varying amounts of molybdenum all of which yield much higher strength than iron alone. In metallurgical terms strength is that property that allows the object to retain its shape under a load; ie. a steel plate not buckling when a weight has been placed on it.

Here we have about 5 grams of pure molybdenum for your collection.

Title: Aluminum
Body HTML:
This humble metal used to be so difficult to refine that it was used as jewelry back in the 19th century until the Heroult process made it, well, dirt cheap! Today aluminum is second only to iron in industrial use and might even upstage the leader if it were only cheaper to make. And that day might come as the cost of electricity drops with new energy technology.

Our sample’s claim to fame is the heady grade of its purity: 99.996%!

Title: Osmium
Body HTML:
Osmium vies with equally exotic rhenium for rarest stable metal found in nature and with iridium for the title of heaviest. At only 1 part per billion in earth's crust and 75% heavier than lead osmium is something special indeed. While the world mines put out some 3,000 tons of gold every year all the osmium would be smaller than a basketball. To top it off osmium is a beautiful shade of light blue! Considering all these factors we suspect the day is not long in coming when someone very rich decides to make jewelry from it. Here's your chance to own a little of this wonderful metal encased in a little cube filled with gel. As you turn the cube the little osmium pieces slowly tumble and turn into new positions - fun!

This container will come with one gram of pure osmium metal.

Title: Barium 99.9%
Body HTML:
Compounds made from barium can be exceptionally toxic. In countries with not-so-strict regulations, barium carbonate is used as a rat poison. The effects are so horrid that you almost feel bad for the pests as it basically gives them an accelerated form of Parkinson’s disease. Within minutes of ingestion their guts are wrecked, their muscles stop responding to the brain and begin twitching of their own volition, their kidneys are shot, then their heartbeats become irregular and several other Bad Things before the merciful end comes from respiratory failure. Medical literature speaks of cases where the white powder was mistaken for flour and… well, let’s move on.

On the other hand each and every day thousands of people in hospitals all over the world are made to drink up Slurpee-sized cups of a vile concoction of barium sulphate (tastes exactly what you would think a liquified chalk smoothie would taste like) prior to getting an abdominal x-ray. Vile but harmless. The offending chemical passes through uneventfully. Well, except for the retching and the memories that will never go away.

So how come? Well, it all hinges on how the metal gets into an organism and what it does once it’s inside. Barium carbonate is water soluble while the sulphate isn’t. Big difference. While the sulphate is indegistible, the gastric juices attack the carbonate and turn it into a chloride which easily passes through intestine walls. Once in the bloodstream barium ions ravage the body’s stores of potassium rapidly depleting this essential electrolyte. This in turn causes all the cascade of nerve and muscle problems described above.

Metallic barium like in this ampule is not a health concern. If the glass were to break - by some unspeakable catastrophe - and your beautiful specimen exposed nothing much would happen even if you were to pick it up with your bare hands. Holding it for a long time might irritate your skin (a chemical burn) but after you washed your hands and said good-bye to your expensive collectible that would be the end of it. Where it does become a much bigger danger is if a piece were to be eaten as, again, the acids in your stomach would make short work of it and turn it into a potentially lethal poison. For this reason, make sure that all of your element samples are well out of reach of little kids.

Title: Uranium
Body HTML:
Nature's alphabet ends with the letter U. The Strong Nuclear Force, the far less well known sister to gravity and electromagnetism, is responsible for sheepherding protons and neutrons into tidy family units. But the SNF's mojo can only carry things so far. Once a family grows too big things can get ugly and relations can break down. Explosively so. There is no magic number but in the case of 235, well, that happens to be an especially troublesome number of family members corraled into one condo which we'll call an "atom". You see, when a dispute breaks out in Uranium clan 235 the violence can break out into neighboring atoms and all hell breaks loose.

Oddly, if a couple more cousins show up things aren't so bad. Uranium-238, otherwise known as depleted uranium or "DU", is of the biggest "pretty stable" atoms that strong nuclear force can manage. That means the metal is actually quite low in radioactivity, relatively speaking, and the type of radioactivity it emits is composed of a comparatively heavy 4-man team composed of two protons and two neutrons. In essence, when uranium breaks apart it farts high-speed helium. And that just doesn't hurt much.

A little more seriously, that's not to say DU is harmless. If allowed to turn into airborne dust it will lodge in your lungs and pepper your alveoli like a machine gun with infinite bullets. While they may not hurt much at any one moment the cumulative effect over years can wreck DNA and degrade tissues into cancerous tumors. Not good.

We offer small chips of DU as shown (totalling one gram in this offer). The U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission allows for the sale and transportation of uranium to the public if certain conditions are met; the most pressing of which deals with the maximum allowable quantity any one person may own (1.5kg) and that it may not be exported outside of the country. For full details please see NRC §40.22 If you can live with these limitations then you should definitely consider owning a little of this marvel of nature.

As stated above, we are unable to ship uranium internationally (sorry).

Title: ERIC II Encyclopedia of Roman Imperial Coins by Rasiel Suarez
Body HTML:
Encyclopedia of Roman Imperial Coins, 2nd edition 2010 by Rasiel Suarez. Copy will be numbered and signed by author.

Amazon reviews: https://www.amazon.com/Eric-Encyclopedia-Roman-Imperial-Coins/dp/0976466414#customerReviews

ISBN 978-0976466413

Title: Roman Display Case
Body HTML:
No doubt about it, the most popular ancient coin collection goal is to get one of each of the emperors that ruled the Roman empire from the time of Augustus through its downfall 500 years later. And no doubt about it, this is also a hopeless task. If the cost doesn’t get you then waiting forever until you get the chance at one of the coins of the very rare emperors will conspire to make this goal impossible to finish. But who cares. The fun is all about the fun you will have plugging the holes one after the other and replacing ugly filler ones with the newest prize that you scored at auction!

This wall-mountable display case allows for an absolute stunning presentation for such a set, turning your home or office into a proper gallery. Made from high-grade acrylic, they will never yellow or turn hazy. They are assembled from two separate panels separated by a quarter inch of space allowing for the included holders to latch into. The front facing panel has the cutouts while the back is printed with the names and reign dates of each of the 190 rulers and family members of the emperors (plus Julius Caesar who although not an emperor belongs in this set anyway!)

This is a large and relatively heavy display for which you will need to allocate a good portion of the wall you intend to hang it on. Measures 47 x 39 inches (119x100cm) so just under four feet on the wide side. Includes the mounting hardware and 191 holders. These clam-shell boxes hold the coin inside under tension and appear to float within, making for a very elegant presentation. A sheet of “sticky dots” is included for optionally placing on the rim of the boxes to prevent accidental falling out.

Recommended assembly: place the back panel against the intended wall location. Mark out the ten holes then drill each with a small bit to allow for easier screwing in of the mounting posts. Set both panels together and insert the two top middle screws with spacers. With a helper to assist, hold up both panels to allow alignment. Lightly screw both into the posts sufficiently to allow the panels to hang securely then work in the remaining screws and dividers. Finger tighten all to finish set up.

Ships by UPS in a huge, heavy box weighing nearly 50lbs! Oh, and just in case it’s not obvious… no, it does not come with the coins. You wish.

Title: Byzantine Display Case
Body HTML:
Collectors of Byzantine coins have often felt like the forgotten stepchildren of the ancient coin world. Having one of these on your wall can go a long way in changing that. Fit for a museum, this acrylic display case will be the pantheon to show off your very best coin of each of the reigns of this thousand year empire. While the chances of filling every hole in this virtual penny book is somewhere between zero and zero (ah, Mezezius, where are you?) you sure will have a grand time getting as complete as possible. Or maybe broke is the right word.

Comes complete with screws, spacers and fasteners along with a float box style holder for each of the 94 people who ruled or thought themselves the ruler of Constantinople. Measures 36x28” (91x72cm). Ships UPS ground to lower 48.

Title: Kings & Queens of England Display Case
Body HTML:
This display case consists of two acrylic panels which when assembled and mounted allows for a permanent housing of a British monarch set coin collection. There is a slot for each of the kings and queens from the first king of all England through to the present (long live Charles because a new slot will be awkward!). Comes with a floating membrane clamshell for each slot that holds a coin of up to 35mm in diameter. Easy to place and remove. Also includes the mounting hardware.

If you are in the UK (or anywhere outside of the U.S.) please check this listing on eBay because it will probably be cheaper.

Measures 91 x 50 x 4 cm

Title: Wheat Pennies Display Case
Body HTML:
This is a penny holder made from laser-cut acrylic sheets with cutouts for every Wheat cent issued from 1909 through 1958 (not including 1922 no D, 1955 double die or other varieties). A front panel, which is also included, can be optionally installed to prevent pennies from falling out and to protect them from tarnishing.

Can be set up as a tabletop display or wall mounted with the included hardware (four standoffs, spacers and screws).

Which pennies does it come with? Uh, none. Go get your own bro 😅

Title: Uranyl Acetate
Body HTML:
If you’ve ever marveled at the incredibly sharp, alien-looking images of a virus or a cell’s internal machinery, you’ve likely been looking at the handiwork of Uranyl Acetate. In the world of Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), this stuff is essentially the "secret sauce." Because uranium atoms are so massive, they are excellent at scattering electrons. When a biological sample is "stained" with this salt, the uranyl ions stick to lipids and proteins, creating the high-contrast shadows needed to see structures that are otherwise invisible. It’s a coordination polymer that has reigned as microscopy’s MVP since the mid-20th century.

But for the element collector, the real magic happens when you turn off the lights. Uranyl Acetate is famous for its intense, ethereal yellow-green fluorescence under ultraviolet light. While labs are slowly moving toward non-radioactive alternatives (using things like gadolinium or samarium), they often lack the "punch" and historical pedigree of the real deal. Our samples are derived from depleted uranium, making them a safe but storied addition to your actinide shelf. It’s a classic lab legend that looks just as good under a blacklight as it does in a history book. Just remember to admire it through the glass this isn't the kind of "glow-up" you want on your skin!

Title: Uranyl Sulfate
Body HTML:
While its cousin, Uranyl Acetate, gets to have all the fun in the biology labs, Uranyl Sulfate was busy doing the heavy lifting during the most intense years of the Cold War. This dense, lemon-yellow salt was a primary workhorse in the "atomic alchemy" pipeline, serving as a key soluble form of uranium for chemical processing. However, its most fascinating claim to fame is its role in the Homogeneous Reactor Experiments (HRE) at Oak Ridge National Laboratory. Unlike traditional reactors that use solid fuel rods, these experimental "liquid fuel" reactors dissolved Uranyl Sulfate directly into water, which acted as both the fuel and the moderator. It was a bold, elegant design that felt like something out of a sci-fi novel.

For the sophisticated collector, Uranyl Sulfate represents the "industrial soul" of the Manhattan Project era. It isn’t quite as flashy as some of the fluorescent oxides, but its historical weight is immense. It represents the transition from raw ore to the refined power that defined the 20th century. If you’re looking for a sample that anchors an actinide collection with true scientific gravitas, this is the one. It’s an understated powerhouse that tells a story of engineering brilliance and the hottest days of the Cold War. Secure a sample for your own "research department" before our stock undergoes its own vanishing act!

Title: Thorium Dioxide
Body HTML:
Thorium Dioxide (or Thoria) is a substance that literally helped light up the world. While the great chemist Jöns Jacob Berzelius named the element after Thor, the Norse god of thunder, in 1829, it took a few more decades for it to become a household staple. In 1885, Carl Auer von Welsbach patented the "gas mantle," a silk mesh soaked in a mixture of 99% thorium dioxide and 1% cerium dioxide. When heated by a flame, the thoria would incandesce with a brilliant, steady white light that far outshone the flickering gas lamps of the era. It was a billion unit success that brought reliable light to the masses before the electric bulb took over.

But the story doesn't stop at lighting. Thoria is incredibly refractory, meaning it has one of the highest melting points of any oxide (3,300 degrees celcius!). This makes it indispensable for TIG welding electrodes and high-end camera lenses, where its high refractive index allows for thinner glass with less distortion. It even had a controversial stint as "Thorotrast," a medical X-ray contrast agent, before doctors realized that internalizing alpha-emitters was a bad idea (yikes!). Today, it’s the "holy grail" for fans of the Thorium Fuel Cycle, representing a future of safer, greener nuclear power. Whether you’re a fan of Victorian-era tech or 21st-century energy, this "history in a bottle" is a must-have trophy.

Title: Thorium Nitrate
Body HTML:
If Thorium Dioxide is the finished product, Thorium Nitrate is the "precursor extraordinaire." This highly soluble salt is the primary starting point for almost everything involving thorium. Since the turn of the century, it has been used to create the thorium-impregnated mantles we mentioned earlier, but it also has a surprisingly "weird" history. In the 1920s, a German company produced "Doramad," a radioactive toothpaste containing thorium nitrate. The marketing claimed it would "massage the gums with radioactive rays"—a reminder of a time when radioactivity was seen as a health tonic rather than a hazard! (snort! snort!)

Beyond the dubious dental history, Thorium Nitrate is at the cutting edge of modern nuclear physics. It is the key ingredient in research for "breeding" Uranium-233. Countries like India, which have massive thorium reserves, are using this chemistry to pave the way for a three-stage nuclear program that could provide carbon-free power for centuries. It’s a reactive, fascinating riddle of a compound that thorium geeks absolutely adore. It’s not a sample for the casual hobbyist; it’s a connoisseur’s piece that bridges the gap between atomic-age nostalgia and the very real future of global energy. It’s the "nitro-boost" your collection needs to reach the next level of actinide expertise!

Title: Periodic Table Elements Card Deck – 118 Elements – Photographic Cards – Educational Chemistry Set – Theodore Gray
Body HTML:
This photographic card deck features all 118 elements of the periodic table, presented as a visually engaging and educational companion for students, collectors, and anyone with an interest in chemistry.

Each card showcases a high quality photograph of the element along with key scientific details, making it an excellent tool for learning, reference, or casual exploration. Whether you're just starting an element collection or looking to deepen your understanding, this set offers an accessible way to interact with the periodic table beyond traditional formats.

Designed with both education and curiosity in mind, the deck is especially well-suited for younger collectors and students. It also makes a thoughtful gift for anyone interested in science, STEM learning, or the elements.

This is a great complement to the physical elements helping bring ad added dimension to anyone’s collection.

Highlights:

  • Complete set covering all 118 elements

  • High-quality photographic cards with scientific information

  • Ideal for students, educators, and new collectors

  • Compact, hands-on learning format

  • Pairs well with physical element samples

Title: Luciteria Science Hat
Body HTML:
A subtle nod to the element collector mindset.

This low-profile cap keeps things simple and functional—unstructured, comfortable, and easy to wear whether you're in the lab, at your desk, or out in the field hunting your next addition. It’s designed to feel broken-in from day one, with a curved visor and adjustable strap for a dialed-in fit.

Made from durable chino cotton twill (with a polyester blend on the camo variant), it’s built for everyday wear without overcomplicating things—just like a well-curated collection.

Details:
• 100% chino cotton twill
• Green camo: 35% chino cotton twill, 65% polyester
• Unstructured, 6-panel, low-profile fit
• 6 embroidered eyelets for breathability
• 3 ⅛” crown
• Adjustable strap with antique buckle

Understated, practical, and quietly distinctive—like the best pieces in any collection.

Title: Cesium 50mm Lucite Cube
Body HTML:
Cesium is perhaps the most sought after element for those looking to complete a set of the periodic table. That's probably because, like gallium, you can melt it in your hand. Except you wouldn't do that because you would melt your hand off, too, in a strangely beautiful but all too tragic purple flame. However, holding the cesium safely with a little glass wall in between is another story. Much prettier than gallium, the brassy color and the sheer difficulty of locating the stuff heighten the appeal and wow factor.

Cesium metal ampoule with one gram of 99.99+ purity embedded in a 2x2 inch (50x50mm) block of crystal clear Lucite acrylic.

Title: Copper 50mm Lucite Cube
Body HTML:
Copper is one of the easiest elements to find. A collector just starting out on a one-of-each element set will fill that Cu hole on the first day with any handy piece of wire or other knickknack. Where copper goes exotic is in the super high purity varieties like so-called "6N" stuff; as in six nines: 99.9999% pure. This level of purity is used in audio hi-fi wiring and the amount of work that goes into removing any trace of impurity from the copper makes it far costlier than ordinary commercial grades. An even higher level of copper, 99.999999 or 8N, is available for those who can't tolerate the indecency of one contaminating atom out of a million. The cost? Oh, $20,000 per meter of wire!!

Anyway, for the rest of us with humbler budgets this specimen of copper is far more affordable and, dare we say, more impressive than wire. This highly polished copper disc was meant to be used in the internals of a scientific apparatus as a laser mirror but, instead, it will decorate a collector's desk - and we predict your friends will all want to see how they look bathed in the beautiful amber light it reflects!

Title: Lanthanum 50mm Lucite Cube
Body HTML: Of the rare earths Lanthanum proves to be one of the easier metals to find - if you don't mind owning a cloudy oxidized piece swimming in oil, that is. The metal is normally shipped in canisters filled with mineral oil. The mineral oil is a good preservative but a thin layer on top of the metal spalls off as whitish lanthanum oxide and leaves behind a more stable form of the oxide that prevents the metal underneath from further corrosion. While irrelevant in terms of its use in the laboratory it's not very aesthetically pleasing. To get shiny lanthanum, small pieces are taken straight from the distillation chamber and placed in an inert atmosphere which fully prevents any oxidation. The samples we sell are stored in argon, in ampoules which are then encased in a 2x2 inch (50x50mm) block of crystal clear Lucite acrylic.

Title: Magnesium 50mm Lucite Cube
Body HTML:
For those who hunt down specimens of pure elements magnesium doesn't pose much of a challenge even if it's not something you can find at Walmart. Widely used in industry as an alloying agent, and also a lab staple for a wide variety of chemistry applications, it is most often sold as ribbons or powdered metal. Alloyed with aluminum it becomes a very light, yet strong metal. For this reason it's often picked as the choice material for the priciest electronics such as professional cameras and luxury laptops. 

Our 50mm acrylic cube holds within a piece of 99.99% pure magnesium metal which has been prepared in such a way as to expose an uneven, roughened texture resembling giant popcorn or metal clouds. As all the other cubes we offer, it will look great as a solitary showpiece or as part of a full collection in the large display case that we offer separately.

Title: Yttrium 50mm Lucite Cube
Body HTML:
Despite what Wikipedia says, yttrium is neither soft nor silvery. In hardness it is similar to iron with both rating around 4 on the Mohs scale. In color, freshly cut yttrium has a neutral gray color similar to iron that dulls to deep gray over the course of several months. 

This metal feels lonely in its assigned spot on the periodic table. It would rather be with its cousins down by the rare earths with which it's always associated in nature. For a collector, yttrium is considered a medium difficulty element to acquire. It is not expensive but it's not something you'd find on grocery shelves either.

This is the Boss of yttrium samples. An ounce of this light metal means it takes up a major portion of the 5cm cube and makes for a truly impressive display. Also available in a smaller piece of similar texture. Purity is 99.99% in both cases.

Title: Tin 50mm Lucite Cube
Body HTML:
Sure, tin isn't the sexiest metal but it's harder to find than you might think given that "tin cans" are really made from steel. Actually, very few household items contain much of this metal nowadays; its main industrial use being as solder and other alloys.

These dramatic shapes are created by rapidly cooling molten tin; the many folds making for a unique and beautiful texture. As is the case with all other items on this page, the beautifully polished Lucite acrylic cube measures 50mm and is guaranteed to remain optically clear without yellowing or crazing.

Title: Erbium 50mm Lucite Cube
Body HTML:
Erbium is another illustrious member of the Rare Earth Gang. The exceedingly difficult task of separating it from the other rare earths is what makes it rare and expensive. The very few uses that have been found for this metal rest solely in its oxides or alloys. No sculptor has ever set chisel to a block of pure erbium nor jeweler received a commission to make art out of it either. Unless the world someday is host to an eccentric billionaire geek who would like a limited edition all-erbium sports car it's very doubtful anyone will ever make use of this metal either.

Except us.

We would like to make history by making our own little modest shrine to the Metal Gods with this hand cast Lucite cube featuring a commercially rare piece of 99.9%+ pure erbium to be admired by all and sundry!

Title: Radon 50mm Lucite Cube
Body HTML:
Normally we shoot for embedding elements that are at least 99% pure but we make exceptions in a few cases where this isn't practical. Clearly, even if it were possible nobody would want a sizeable sample of pure radon gas. At least we think nobody would want such a thing. Radon gas is responsible for over 20,000 lung cancer deaths in the U.S. alone where it concentrates in the basements of many homes built in regions where there's a lot of granite or shale.

No worries with this sample offered here however. The amount of radon is very small and trapped behind this much acrylic it is fully shielded from causing any harm. The radon comes from a precisely measured amount of thorium dioxide the decay chain of which yields some 0.00000000017 of a gram's worth of radon atoms at any one time. The weight being so slight it's difficult to get a visual of how much radon you're buying but it would probably take up the volume of a grain of sand... with the rest of the ampule being regular air and the powdered ThO2. All, of course, tidily presented in our beautifully finished 5cm Lucite acrylic cube!

Title: Palladium 50mm Lucite Cube
Body HTML:
Luciteria procures a fair amount of material from Russian sources but this Soviet-era piece is by far the most touching we have come across. This coin was minted in the closing days of the Soviet Union and is a series much sought out by coin collectors fascinated in equal measure by the unusual choice of metal as well as the motif. The front gives us a nostalgic look of cold war symbolism; ten Rubles that were never meant to be spent in any Russian store but instead were swiftly whisked out of the country to be sold to Western markets. On the back, appearing like a Degas sketch, a ballerina strikes a pose mid-pirouette under the simple cyrillic banner "Russian Ballet". An incomparable specimen for the element collector who wants the very best representatives for each slot in the periodic table!

Coin weighs 15.5g and is 99.95% pure.

Title: Europium 50mm Lucite Cube
Body HTML: Every element collector looks longingly at that row down near the bottom of the periodic table with the unpronounceable names. None of those slots are easy to fill and the "rare" in rare earths only raises the interest that much more. But as it turns out only a couple of those are actually truly rare. Europium and lutetium are the crown jewels in this neighborhood; Lu for rarity and Eu for its legendary difficulty in getting a tarnish-free specimen. Well, here's your chance at getting your hands on just such a beast. Check out that oxygen-starved glint. You know you want it. A happy 3g of unoxidized europium in argon, embedded in a 2x2 inch (50x50mm) block of crystal clear Lucite acrylic.  Lovingly embedded just for you in the USA.

Title: Calcium 50mm Lucite Cube
Body HTML:
Calcium - known to every schoolkid as some vaguely healthy substance in milk and cheese - is an element whose true nature is much less well known to most people. The metal simply cannot exist in nature uncombined with other elements because it is too reactive; that is, too willing to pair its atoms with other atoms to form molecules.

While in free form it is unknown to so many its molecules sure aren't: CaCO3 gives us both chalk and marble while the slightly different CaSO4 is much better known as the sheet rock that lines most American homes. Your very bones are a brew of calcium phosphates.

Even though you get it from drinking milk and yogurt, people shouldn't be surprised to learn that calcium is a metal. After all, reading through the ingredients list on a cereal box there's iron and magnesium and others too. But the thing is that calcium isn't something most are familiar with as an object.

And yet, it really is a metal. On the moon it would make for a perfect metal to make, I don't know, a cell tower or something. It's very light and plentiful. Why the moon? Well, because there's no atmosphere or water there that could come in contact with our imaginary calcium tower. Either of those touch it and the calcium instantly sizzles and corrodes.

No worries though. Our sample here is stored in argon to keep that delicate luster alive for all eternity. Well, as "eternal" as the cube don't get broke, that is! ;-)

This offering is for one ampoule containing a piece of 99.99% pure calcium metal embedded in a 50x50mm block of crystal clear Lucite acrylic. Hand cast in the USA.

Title: Arsenic 50mm Lucite Cube
Body HTML:
Mention "arsenic" and the first thing that comes to most people's minds is some sort of medieval poison. That's all thanks to arsenic trioxide and the many other compounds it forms, which throughout the ages have been fed to unsuspecting husbands in their soup. After a few weeks of this condiment they would wake up one day to a lethal bout of vomiting and diarrhea.... all the easier to pass off as food poisoning! Regular elemental arsenic is much less notorious. Sometimes it's found in its native state but is typically sold as amorphous gray or black powder.

Getting shiny arsenic is much more difficult because the element promptly oxidizes in open air. To keep it shiny we must deprive it of every last trace of oxygen. This is accomplished by first putting this arsenic in a big fishtank-like glass box that is filled with a noble gas which will not chemically react with it. With sample inside, each glass ampule is then sealed inside this controlled atmosphere and shipped from the laboratory to the caster who turns it into a proper nerd’s trophy!

Title: Samarium 50mm Lucite Cube
Body HTML:
As far as collecting the elements go the only thing that is remarkable about samarium is that it's the cheapest of the sixteen in the rare earths group to acquire. Its one major application is for making strong magnets when alloyed with cobalt. The dendritic distillation with its densely fibrous texture is perhaps the most attractive variant offered.

Samarium metal piece weighing approximately 15 grams and 99.95%+ pure embedded in a 2x2 inch (50x50mm) block of crystal clear Lucite acrylic.

Title: Manganese 50mm Lucite Cube
Body HTML: An industrial metal, manganese is used to render desirable properties to steel such as strength and resistance to corrosion. It would be really cool to be able to buy, say, a spoon made out of pure manganese but there is no such thing. In fact, despite being a rather common metal it is actually only rarely available as a standalone metal at all; and then only typically as powder or unattractive electrolytic "chips" shiny on one side and the other mottled and oxidized. In an altogether different category we find lab created nodules made for studying naturally occurring alloys rich in manganese that are found on the ocean floor. This one is over 99% pure and embedded in a 2 inch block of gorgeous Lucite.

Title: Carbon 50mm Lucite Cube
Body HTML:
Carbon is so interesting. It is found in its humble form of coal and its coveted form of diamonds.
